title: build_diagnostic docstring claims "Always succeeds" but the function has reachable raise paths its sole caller must (and does) handle
proof:
Verified by reading the cited lines and empirically triggering the failure. _enrichment.py:388-389 reads exactly:
```
def build_diagnostic(formula: LTLFormula) -> PropertyDiagnostic:
    """Build a PropertyDiagnostic from a formula. Always succeeds."""
```
The body calls collect_signals(formula) and format_formula(formula). collect_signals -> _walk_formula raises ValidationError when depth > MAX_NESTING_DEPTH (_enrichment.py:130-132); format_formula raises the same at _enrichment.py:285-287, and recurses through _format_predicate -> _format_rational (_enrichment.py:222-237, 195-219) -> _get_or_load_renderer_lib (_enrichment.py:167-192) which calls find_ffi_library() + ctypes.CDLL(). MAX_NESTING_DEPTH = 64 (python/aletheia/limits.py:53). EMPIRICALLY CONFIRMED by running the actual venv python (python/.venv/bin/python3): a formula nested 66 deep makes `build_diagnostic(f)` raise `ValidationError: Formula nesting depth exceeds 64`. The caller _client.py:552-560 wraps the build_diagnostic loop in `except FFIError, ValidationError:` (note: in Python 3.14, PEP 758, the parenthesis-less form means `except (FFIError, ValidationError):`, NOT Python-2 binding) and re-raises after clearing self._diags, and its own docstring (_client.py:531-540) lists FFIError and ValidationError as raised. The "Always succeeds." claim is directly contradicted by the call graph, the empirical raise, and the caller's defensive handling.

CORRECTION to the finder's proof (does not change the verdict): the finder claims the load-failure path raises FFIError. In Python it does NOT. find_ffi_library raises FileNotFoundError (_ffi.py:340) / PermissionError (via _validate_lib_path), and ctypes.CDLL raises OSError; FFIError (a subclass of AletheiaError, NOT OSError, per _types.py:59) is never raised on this path. So the caller's `except FFIError, ...` would actually let a load failure propagate uncaught. The ValidationError path alone — empirically proven — is sufficient to confirm the docstring is a lie.
suggested_fix: Replace "Always succeeds." with an accurate Raises section, e.g. `Raises: ValidationError if formula nesting exceeds MAX_NESTING_DEPTH; FileNotFoundError/OSError if the rational renderer cannot load libaletheia-ffi.so.` Per the project's cross-binding-consistency rule (feedback_cross_binding_consistency_as_fp), apply the same correction to the identical "Always succeeds" claims in the peers — go/aletheia/enrich.go:41 (BuildDiagnostic, where formatRationalFFI PANICS on missing .so per renderer.go:200, so it provably does NOT always succeed, AND the Go caller at client.go:597-598 does not defend), cpp/include/aletheia/enrich.hpp:22 (whose own caller cpp/src/client.cpp:456 comments "build_diagnostic reaches format_rational_ffi which throws AletheiaException(Ffi)"), and rust/src/enrich.rs:33 — and align caller defenses across bindings (Python+C++ catch; Go does not).

title: Documented dbc_to_json ∘ dbc_to_text roundtrip identity is not invocable (path-vs-text type mismatch)
proof: Re-read _converter.py myself. Module docstring lines 13-15: '``dbc_to_json`` ∘ ``dbc_to_text`` is the identity on any well-formed DBC'. dbc_to_text docstring lines 64-65: 'Inverse of :func:`dbc_to_json` at the wire level: ``dbc_to_json(dbc_to_text(d))`` returns ``d`` byte-identical'. The code contradicts this composition: dbc_to_json is declared `def dbc_to_json(dbc_path: str | Path) -> DBCDefinition` (line 25) and its body treats the argument as a filesystem path — line 47 `path = Path(dbc_path)`, line 48 `check_dbc_text_size_bound(path.stat().st_size)`, line 52 `text = path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")`. dbc_to_text returns DBC text content (`def dbc_to_text(dbc: DBCDefinition) -> str`, line 61; body line 82-83 returns `client.format_dbc_text(dbc)`). So `dbc_to_json(dbc_to_text(d))` passes multi-line DBC text where a path is expected; `Path(text).stat()` raises FileNotFoundError — the composition never returns d. grep across python/ shows exactly one `dbc_to_json` (path-only; no text-accepting overload). The repo's own helper test_converter_paths.py:35-39 `_write_valid_dbc` proves the real pattern requires an intermediate file: `path.write_text(dbc_to_text(_sample_dbc()))` THEN `dbc_to_json(path)`. The genuinely invocable string-level inverse pair is client.parse_dbc_text / client.format_dbc_text. The docstring is therefore a self-contradicting lie about an invocation that cannot run.
suggested_fix: Reword both docstrings to describe the actual relationship: dbc_to_text(d) emits text whose subsequent parse yields d — either `dbc_to_json` applied to a FILE containing that text, or the directly-invocable client.parse_dbc_text(client.format_dbc_text(d)). Do not claim `dbc_to_json(dbc_to_text(d))` is identity, since dbc_to_json consumes a path, not text.

title: load_can_log / iter_can_log docstrings claim a 5-tuple return shape; they actually yield 7-field CANFrameTuple
proof: Re-read can_log.py and _types.py myself. load_can_log Returns (line 75-76): 'List of (timestamp_us, arbitration_id, dlc, data, extended) tuples' — 5 fields. iter_can_log Yields (line 104-105): '(timestamp_us, arbitration_id, dlc, data, extended) tuples' — 5 fields. But both return CANFrameTuple: load_can_log -> list[CANFrameTuple] (line 66), iter_can_log -> Iterator[CANFrameTuple] (line 95), and convert_message constructs `CANFrameTuple(timestamp_us, msg.arbitration_id, dlc, data, msg.is_extended_id, brs, esi)` (lines 182-190) — 7 positional fields, with brs/esi computed at lines 179-180. CANFrameTuple in client/_types.py:413-423 declares 7 fields: timestamp, can_id, dlc, data, extended, brs (`bool | None = None`), esi (`bool | None = None`). The module's own example (can_log.py:16) correctly unpacks 7: `for ts, can_id, dlc, data, ext, brs, esi in iter_can_log(...)`. So the Returns/Yields lines under-report the shape and contradict both the code and the in-file example.
suggested_fix: Update both docstrings to 'List/Iterator of CANFrameTuple (timestamp_us, arbitration_id, dlc, data, extended, brs, esi)' to match CANFrameTuple's 7 fields and the module example.

title: Excel template module docstring DBC-sheet column list omits the 'Extended' column the code actually writes and reads
proof: Re-read excel_loader.py myself. Module docstring lines 32-34 document the DBC sheet columns as: 'Message ID | Message Name | DLC | Signal | Start Bit | Length | Byte Order | Signed | Factor | Offset | Min | Max | Unit | Multiplexor | Multiplex Value' — no 'Extended' (15 columns). But DBC_HEADERS (lines 145-162) lists 16 columns with 'Extended' as the 3rd entry (line 148, between 'Message Name' and 'DLC'). The loader reads that cell: _parse_dbc_rows line 603 `ext_val = row.get("Extended")` and line 604 `is_extended = get_bool(row, "Extended", ...) if ext_val is not None else False`. DBC_HEADERS is also written by create_template via _write_header_row(ws_dbc, DBC_HEADERS). The docstring is a stale spec that drifted when the Extended column was added; a technician following it builds a sheet off-by-one against the template/loader.
suggested_fix: Add 'Extended' to the documented DBC column list between 'Message Name' and 'DLC' to match DBC_HEADERS; note it is optional (defaults to False when blank, mirroring lines 603-604) like Multiplexor / Multiplex Value.

title: parse_rational float branch docstring claims a 'float-from-string heuristic' but the code uses Fraction(float) + limit_denominator
proof: Re-read rational.py myself. Docstring lines 176-177: 'we still go through Fraction, using its float-from-string heuristic to avoid binary float artifacts.' The float branch (lines 185-189) does NO string conversion: line 189 `return Fraction(value_raw).limit_denominator(_DECIMAL_PRECISION_DEN)` where _DECIMAL_PRECISION_DEN = 1_000_000_000 (line 27). Fraction(float) yields the exact IEEE-754 binary artifact (e.g. Fraction(0.1) == 3602879701896397/36028797018963968); only .limit_denominator(1e9) collapses it to 1/10. There is no Fraction(str(value_raw)) anywhere in the branch, so the named 'float-from-string heuristic' mechanism does not exist in the code. The result is correct, but the documented mechanism is false.
suggested_fix: Reword to 'converts via Fraction(value) then limit_denominator(1_000_000_000) to collapse the IEEE-754 binary artifact to a small-denominator rational'; drop the inaccurate 'float-from-string heuristic' wording.

title: PropertyResultEntry docstring scopes it to 'end-of-stream', but it is also the mid-stream property_batch element type
proof: Re-read types.py and _response_parsers.py myself. types.py line 600-601: `class PropertyResultEntry(TypedDict): """A single property finalization result at end-of-stream."""`. But the same type is the element type of the MID-STREAM per-frame batch: PropertyBatchResponse (line 579, docstring line 580 'Per-frame batch of property events emitted during streaming') declares `results: list[PropertyResultEntry]` (line 597). And _response_parsers.py parses mid-stream property_batch entries into this exact type: line 200 `parsed_results: list[PropertyResultEntry]` filled by `_parse_property_event` (line 215, returns PropertyResultEntry) within the property_batch branch (lines 189-206). So the type spans both mid-stream per-frame events and end-of-stream finalization; the 'at end-of-stream' qualifier under-scopes it and would mislead a reader handling mid-stream events.
suggested_fix: Generalize the opening line, e.g. 'A single property result entry — used both for mid-stream property_batch events and for end-of-stream finalization results.'

title: FloatToRational integer fast-path silently wraps 2^63 to MinInt64 instead of erroring (overflow error-path missing; violates own docstring + Python/C++ parity)
proof: Re-verified types.go:84-86: `if v == math.Trunc(v) && v >= math.MinInt64 && v <= math.MaxInt64 { return Rational{Numerator: int64(v), Denominator: 1}, nil }`. Empirically (Go program I ran): at v=2^63 (9223372036854775808.0): v==Trunc(v)=true, v>=MinInt64=true, v<=MaxInt64=true (because float64(math.MaxInt64) rounds UP to exactly 2^63, so the float comparison passes), and int64(v) = -9223372036854775808 (MinInt64 — sign-corrupted wrap), returned with err==nil. This violates the docstring at types.go:69-79 ('returns an error for ... values that overflow int64 when scaled ... use this at user-input boundaries ... where silently mishandling a bad value would mask data entry mistakes') and NeverExceeds' docstring at check.go:77-78 ('Returns an error if value is ... overflows int64 when scaled to a rational (matching the Python and C++ bindings, which raise/throw rather than silently clamping a malformed value to 0/1)'). Reachability confirmed: check.go funnels every builder (NeverExceeds/NeverBelow/StaysBetween/Equals/SettlesBetween) through exported FloatToRational (check.go:80,94,109,113,130,145,203,207,244,251,258,282,294,308,309), and the Excel loader does too (go/excel/excel.go:864). CORRECTION to the finder's window claim: I empirically determined the buggy input is EXACTLY the single point v=2^63; the next representable double below it (9223372036854774784) casts correctly, and the negative boundary (MinInt64) is fine. So it is a single-boundary-value defect, NOT a wide range across every value. The finder's separate claim that RationalFromFloat (types.go:61-67) is also poisoned is REFUTED for this input: RationalFromFloat calls FloatToRational and panics only when err!=nil; since FloatToRational returns err==nil here, RationalFromFloat does NOT panic — it returns the same wrong value (a different, milder symptom than 'promises to panic but returns a wrong value' — it makes no panic promise for inputs FloatToRational accepts).
suggested_fix: Add a post-cast round-trip check to the integer fast-path, matching the C++ idiom (cpp/src/types.cpp:27-29 casts first then checks `static_cast<double>(as_int) == d`). Replace `v <= math.MaxInt64` gating with: compute `n := int64(v)` then accept only if `float64(n) == v`; otherwise fall through to the scaling path (which uses the strict `limit = math.MaxInt64/rationalDenominator - 1` and errors). Python (python/aletheia/client/_helpers/rational.py:44-51) has no integer fast-path at all (always 10^9-scales with exact `_INT64_MAX = (1<<63)-1` bound) and is correct; C++ uses the safe round-trip check and is correct; only Go diverges.

title: MessageByID/MessageByName doc claims a deep copy 'mutating it does not affect the DBCDefinition', but copyMessage aliases Senders and per-signal Receivers/ValueDescriptions slices
proof: Re-verified. Doc at dbc.go:635 (MessageByID) and dbc.go:653 (MessageByName): 'The returned pointer is a deep copy; mutating it does not affect the DBCDefinition.' copyMessage at dbc.go:671-675 is exactly `out := *m; out.Signals = append([]DBCSignal(nil), m.Signals...); return &out`. The shallow struct copy `out := *m` makes out.Senders (dbc.go:58, []string) alias m.Senders. The append rebuilds the Signals backing array, but each DBCSignal element is copied by value, so each element's Receivers (dbc.go:40, []string) and ValueDescriptions (dbc.go:45, []DBCValueEntry) slice headers still point to the SAME backing arrays as the stored definition. I confirmed empirically with a probe test against the real package: after `got := def.MessageByID(id); got.Senders[0]=...; got.Signals[0].Receivers[0]=...; got.Signals[0].ValueDescriptions[0].Description=...`, a re-fetch `def.MessageByID(id)` returned all three MUTATED values (test logged 'CONFIRMED: Senders aliased', 'signal Receivers aliased', 'signal ValueDescriptions aliased'), then I removed the probe. Note SignalByName at dbc.go:192-195 correctly documents its copy as shallow ('The copy is shallow: slice fields ... still alias the originals'); MessageByID/MessageByName over-promise relative to that honest sibling. (Minor: SignalByName's doc names the field 'ValueDescs' while the struct field is 'ValueDescriptions' — a separate stale-name nit, not part of this finding.)
suggested_fix: Either make copyMessage truly deep (also `out.Senders = append([]string(nil), m.Senders...)` and, for each copied signal, copy Receivers and ValueDescriptions and rebuild signalIndex), or correct both doc comments to state the copy is shallow for Senders and per-signal slice fields, matching SignalByName's honest caveat.

title: extractCache doc references a non-existent Client.mu field; the Client serializes via lockCh (channel semaphore), not a mutex
proof: Re-verified. enrich.go:296: '// (protected by Client.mu).' and enrich.go:303: '// Client.mu whenever it reads or writes entries.' The Client struct (client.go:64-83) declares `lockCh chan struct{}` (line 67), `closeOnce sync.Once`, `closed bool`, `lockWaiters atomic.Int32`, and others — NO `mu` field. Its own doc (client.go:49-63) explicitly states lockCh is the serializing primitive and that sync.Mutex 'has no context-cancellable variant'. grep across the non-test package shows `Client.mu` appears ONLY in those two enrich.go comments; the only `mu` field in the package is MockBackend.mu (mock.go:29), an unrelated type. The cache is in fact guarded by lockCh (correct behavior), so this is a stale/false comment only — no runtime defect. Low severity is appropriate.
suggested_fix: Replace 'Client.mu' with 'the Client's lockCh semaphore' (or 'the client lock acquired via Client.lock') in both enrich.go comments (lines 296 and 303) so the documented invariant names the real serializing primitive.

title: Error-code doc says 'eight groups' but the parenthetical enumerates only seven group names
proof: Re-verified. error.go:64-66: '// Constants below are organized into eight groups (Parse / DBC text / // Frame / Route / Handler / Dispatch / Extraction); ...'. The parenthetical lists seven names: Parse(1), DBC text(2), Frame(3), Route(4), Handler(5), Dispatch(6), Extraction(7). Reading the actual const block (error.go:69-199), there are eight blank-line-delimited groups: Parse (CodeParse*, 70-107), DBC text (CodeDBCText*, 109-114), Frame (CodeFrame*, 116-132), an Input-bound group of one (CodeInputBoundExceeded, 134-140, blank-line-separated on both sides between Frame and Route), Route (CodeRoute*, 142-159), Handler (CodeHandler*, 161-178), Dispatch (CodeDispatch*, 180-187), Extraction (CodeExtraction*, 189-198). The stated count 'eight' matches the eight actual groups, but the parenthetical omits the Input-bound group (CodeInputBoundExceeded). So the count and the listed names disagree — exactly as the finder claims. Comment-only; low severity correct. Cross-binding: no peer (C++/Python/Rust) structures its error-code docs as 'N groups', so this is Go-only.
suggested_fix: Add the missing 'Input-bound' group to the parenthetical (making eight names: Parse / DBC text / Frame / Input-bound / Route / Handler / Dispatch / Extraction) so the enumeration matches the stated count and the actual blank-line grouping in the const block.

title: MessageByID/MessageByName/copyMessage claim "deep copy / mutating does not affect" but the copy is shallow at the signal level
proof: Re-read dbc.go:670-675: copyMessage does `out := *m; out.Signals = append([]DBCSignal(nil), m.Signals...); return &out`. `out := *m` copies the struct shallowly, so out.Senders ([]string, dbc.go:58) shares the source's backing array. The append copies DBCSignal structs by value but their slice fields — Receivers []string (dbc.go:40), ValueDescriptions []DBCValueEntry (dbc.go:45) — and Presence's Multiplexed.MultiplexValues []MultiplexValue (dbc.go:19) all keep aliasing the originals. So `m := d.MessageByID(id); m.Signals[0].Receivers[0] = "X"` mutates d.Messages[idx].Signals[0].Receivers[0], contradicting the doc "The returned pointer is a deep copy; mutating it does not affect the DBCDefinition" (dbc.go:635, dbc.go:653) and "deep copy of a DBCMessage, including its Signals slice" (dbc.go:670). The sibling SignalByName comment (dbc.go:193-195) honestly states the per-signal copy is shallow, confirming the author knew. The Python peer's docstring (python/aletheia/dbc/_queries.py message_by_id) explicitly says "Unlike the Go binding the returned dict is a reference ... mutating it mutates the underlying definition" — i.e. Python documents Go as THE deep-copy binding, so Go's broken contract also makes the cross-binding documentation wrong.
suggested_fix: Either implement a true deep copy in copyMessage (clone each signal's Receivers, ValueDescriptions and Multiplexed.MultiplexValues, plus the message's Senders) to honor the deep-copy contract, or downgrade the MessageByID/MessageByName/copyMessage comments to state the copy is shallow at the nested-slice level (matching SignalByName at dbc.go:193-195). Note Python's _queries.py docstring names Go as the deep-copy binding, so fixing the implementation is the parity-correct option.

title: Package "Observability event vocabulary" enumerates 15 events, omitting endstream.uncached_atom which the code emits and the spec mandates
proof: Re-read doc.go:65-86. The block says the Client/FFIBackend "emit structured records with the following event names ... any drift here is a finding" then lists exactly 15 names (rts.cores_mismatch, dbc.parsed, properties.set, stream.started, stream.ended, error_event.sent, remote_event.sent, frame.processed, cache.hit, cache.miss, cache.full, enrichment.property_index_oob, enrichment.extraction_failed, extraction.process_failed, extraction.parse_failed — counted 15 via grep). client.go:964 emits a 16th: `c.logger.LogAttrs(ctx, slog.LevelWarn, "endstream.uncached_atom", ...)`. PROTOCOL.md:1359 mandates "the same 16-event vocabulary" and PROTOCOL.md:1368 lists endstream.uncached_atom explicitly. The C++ peer emits it (cpp/src/client.cpp:670) and the Python peer's LogEvent enum docstring (python/aletheia/client/_log.py:38) states "Exactly 16 values — the set must stay identical across bindings." So the Go doc's self-declared exhaustive list is short by exactly the event it forbids drifting on.
suggested_fix: Add `endstream.uncached_atom   (Client, Warn)` to the vocabulary block in doc.go so the documented list matches the 16-event canonical vocabulary emitted at client.go:964 and mandated by PROTOCOL.md:1359-1368. Python's LogEvent enum (16 values) is the correct reference.

title: Comment says "(51 codes)" but the file defines 57 Code* constants (and the same stale count appears in the C++ peer)
proof: Re-read error.go:61-62: "Machine-readable error codes matching Agda Error ADT (51 codes). Each maps 1:1 to an Agda error constructor via errorCode." `grep -cE '^\s*Code[A-Za-z]+ = "' go/aletheia/error.go` returns 57, broken down by prefix as Parse(19)/DBCText(3)/Frame(7)/Input(1)/Route(9)/Handler(9)/Dispatch(4)/Extraction(5) = 57. The file's own constant list internally falsifies the "51". This is a descriptive-accuracy error with no caller-observable behavior (no code path reads the count), hence low not medium. Cross-binding: the C++ peer carries the SAME stale count — cpp/include/aletheia/error.hpp:39-40 says "the ADT defines 51 codes across 6 families" — so the drift is in multiple bindings; Python (python/aletheia/codes/_error.py:21-23) wisely carries no literal count.
suggested_fix: Replace "(51 codes)" with the correct count, or — to avoid recurring drift — drop the literal and say "one constant per code emitted by Aletheia.Error.errorCode". Apply the same fix to cpp/include/aletheia/error.hpp:39-40 (also stale at 51).

title: Comment says constants are organized into "eight groups" but the parenthetical lists only seven (omits the Input bound group)
proof: Re-read error.go:64-65: "Constants below are organized into eight groups (Parse / DBC text / Frame / Route / Handler / Dispatch / Extraction)". The parenthetical enumerates seven items (Parse, DBC text, Frame, Route, Handler, Dispatch, Extraction) but the sentence says eight. The body genuinely has eight families — CodeInputBoundExceeded sits in its own block at error.go:134-140 (blank-line-separated between the Frame and Route groups) and is not named by any of the seven. The prefix grouping confirms eight: Parse(19), DBCText(3), Frame(7), Input(1), Route(9), Handler(9), Dispatch(4), Extraction(5). So the stated count (8) and the listed names (7) self-contradict. Low: descriptive-only, no caller harm. Single-binding: the C++ peer (error.hpp:39-40) has a different but internally-consistent mismatch (says "6 families" and lists 6 names), so the count-vs-listed-names self-contradiction is specific to the Go comment.
suggested_fix: Add the missing group: "(Parse / DBC text / Frame / Input bound / Route / Handler / Dispatch / Extraction)" so the listed names match the stated count of eight.

title: validateTimeBound doc claims it rejects int64 overflow but the body only checks negativity
proof: Re-read json.go:487-494. Doc: "validateTimeBound rejects TimeBounds the Agda core cannot represent (negative microseconds or overflow past int64 in the wire payload)." Body: `if t.Microseconds < 0 { return validationError(...) }; return nil` — the sole check is `< 0`. TimeBound.Microseconds is declared int64 (types.go:106-108), so every value it can hold is by definition representable in int64; an out-of-int64-range wire value would already fail at JSON unmarshal before reaching this function. The promised "overflow past int64" rejection is therefore neither implemented nor possible. Low: the missing clause is a no-op, so no caller is misled into unsafe behavior. Single-binding: the int64-field-cannot-overflow-int64 framing is specific to this Go function/type.
suggested_fix: Drop the "or overflow past int64" clause from the comment; the int64-typed field cannot overflow int64, leaving only the negative-microseconds rejection the body actually performs.

title: extractCache docs reference a nonexistent Client.mu field for the synchronization contract
proof: Re-read enrich.go:294-306. enrich.go:295-296: "all access must be synchronized by the caller (protected by Client.mu)." enrich.go:302-303: "the Client holds Client.mu whenever it reads or writes entries." The Client struct (client.go:64-83) has no mu field — it declares `lockCh chan struct{}` (client.go:67) and `closeOnce sync.Once` (client.go:68); `grep -nE '^\s*mu\b' client.go` returns nothing. The only `mu sync.Mutex` in the package is on MockBackend (mock.go:29) and in test files. The actual serialization primitive is the channel semaphore lockCh, so the documented synchronization contract names a primitive that does not exist on Client. Low: the described intent (single-threaded access enforced by the Client's lock) is correct; only the identifier is wrong. Single-binding: Go-specific identifier.
suggested_fix: Replace "Client.mu" in both comments with "the Client's internal lock (lockCh / the lock()/unlock() pair)" so the named primitive matches the one that actually exists (client.go:67).

title: mockSentinel comment references a nonexistent Client.process method
proof: Re-read mock.go:92-94: "...and Client.process checks c.closed rather than c.state for the use-after-close guard." The Client method is processLocked (client.go:188 `func (c *Client) processLocked(input string) (string, error)`), whose body does `if c.closed { return "", stateError("client is closed") }` (client.go:189). `grep -nE 'func \(c \*Client\) process\b' client.go` returns nothing — no method named process exists. The described behavior (closed-check on c.closed, not c.state) is accurate; only the identifier is stale. Low; single-binding (Go-specific identifier).
suggested_fix: Update the comment to name Client.processLocked (client.go:188) instead of Client.process.

title: SignalByName comment names a nonexistent field ValueDescs (actual field is ValueDescriptions)
proof: Re-read dbc.go:192-195: the doc describes the shallow-copy aliasing for slice fields "(`Receivers`, `ValueDescs`)". DBCSignal declares Receivers []string (dbc.go:40) and ValueDescriptions []DBCValueEntry (dbc.go:45); there is no field named ValueDescs. `grep -rn 'ValueDescs' go/aletheia/*.go` finds it only inside this comment (other hits are the unrelated local var unresolvedValueDescs and the field UnresolvedValueDescriptions in json.go). So the comment references an identifier that does not exist on the type it documents. Low; single-binding (Go-specific field name).
suggested_fix: Rename `ValueDescs` to `ValueDescriptions` in the SignalByName comment (dbc.go:194) to match the actual DBCSignal field at dbc.go:45.

title: parseYAMLChecks comment measures cost "per workbook" — wrong unit-of-work term for a YAML-document decoder
proof: Re-read yaml.go:151-156: "parseYAMLChecks decodes a YAML document into a list of CheckResults. ... Cost is negligible on the loader path (microseconds per workbook); benefit is actionable diagnostics." The function operates on YAML bytes via yaml.Unmarshal (yaml.go:161 and yaml.go:175); its unit of work is a YAML document, not a workbook. "Workbook" is an Excel/.xlsx concept living in go/excel (e.g. go/excel/excel.go:80,111,135,162) — `grep -rn 'workbook' go/aletheia/*.go` finds it in non-test code ONLY at yaml.go:156. So the comment uses the wrong unit-of-work term. (I dropped the finder's "copy-paste of Excel-loader wording" provenance claim: there is no verbatim 'microseconds per workbook' twin in excel.go, so provenance is unverifiable — but the misnomer itself is real and proven.) Low; single-binding.
suggested_fix: Replace "per workbook" with "per document" (or "per YAML file") in the parseYAMLChecks comment at yaml.go:156.

title: MessageByID / MessageByName claim a "deep copy" but per-signal slices and message Senders still alias the original
proof: Re-read dbc.go. copyMessage (670-675): `out := *m; out.Signals = append([]DBCSignal(nil), m.Signals...); return &out`. `out := *m` bit-copies the struct, so `out.Senders` (a []string slice header, field declared at 58) aliases m.Senders' backing array. The reslice gives a fresh []DBCSignal backing array, but each element is a struct copy whose Receivers []string (field 40) and ValueDescriptions []DBCValueEntry (field 45) slice headers still point at the originals. So a caller doing `returned.Signals[0].Receivers[0] = "X"` or `returned.Senders[0] = "X"` mutates d.Messages[idx]. The docs at line 635 (MessageByID) and 653 (MessageByName) both state "The returned pointer is a deep copy; mutating it does not affect the DBCDefinition." — contradicted by the body. The honest sibling SignalByName (192-195) documents the OPPOSITE for the identical copy mechanism: "The copy is shallow: slice fields (Receivers, ValueDescs) on the returned signal still alias the originals; mutating the returned signal's slices mutates the parent message's signals." The two doc surfaces are mutually contradictory. (Note: copyMessage also bit-copies the unexported signalIndex map header, so the returned message shares the index map too — a further undocumented alias, not the documented contradiction.)
suggested_fix: Either make copyMessage a true deep copy (clone Senders and each signal's Receivers and ValueDescriptions slices) to honor the MessageByID/MessageByName docs, or change those docs to match SignalByName's honest shallow-aliasing wording. Verified the C++ peer does NOT make this promise: cpp/include/aletheia/dbc.hpp:389-390 returns `const DbcMessage*` (const access into the definition, no copy), so this is a Go-only doc/behavior contradiction.

title: then-clause condition dispatch is duplicated between yaml.go and go/excel/excel.go (no shared DispatchThen, breaking the DispatchSimple/DispatchWhen precedent)
proof: Re-read both. yaml.go parseYAMLWhenThen 297-318 switches then.Condition over equals -> thenBuilder.Equals(...).Within; exceeds -> thenBuilder.Exceeds(...).Within; stays_between -> thenBuilder.StaysBetween(...).Within; default -> error. go/excel/excel.go parseWhenThenRow 497-537 has the same three cases with the same builder calls (Equals/Exceeds/StaysBetween + Within) and the same Then-Min/Then-Max required-field validation. The shared-helper precedent is real: loader.go DispatchSimple (54-70) and DispatchWhen (72-86) were extracted and exported precisely so external loaders share the vocabulary (loader.go 72-74 doc states this). The missing DispatchThen is a genuine break of that established pattern. DOWNGRADED to low because (a) the two legs are in-sync TODAY (no live skew), and (b) they consume different input shapes — typed YAML structs (entry.Then.Value as *float) vs xlsx string maps extracted via xlsxNumber/xlsxInt — so the duplication is only the 3-way condition->builder dispatch, not the whole leg. CORRECTED the finder's file path: the Excel loader is go/excel/excel.go (a separate Go module importing the aletheia package), NOT go/aletheia/excel.go which does not exist.
suggested_fix: Add an exported DispatchThen(builder ThenSignalBuilder, condition string, value/min/max already-extracted) (ThenCondition, error) in loader.go mirroring DispatchWhen, and route both the YAML (297-318) and Excel (497-537) sites through it so a new then-condition is a one-file change. This is a Go-only DRY item; the C++/Python loaders do not share this Go-specific Dispatch* extraction pattern.

title: MockBackend binary shims pass state inconsistently (some Process(nil,...), some Process(state,...))
proof: Re-read mock.go. SendFrameBinary (143) `return m.Process(nil, "<binary:sendFrame>")`; SendErrorBinary (148) `m.Process(nil, "<binary:sendError>")`; SendRemoteBinary (153) `m.Process(nil, "<binary:sendRemote>")`. vs StartStreamBinary (158) `m.Process(state, "<binary:startStream>")`, EndStreamBinary (163), FormatDBCBinary (168), ExtractSignalsBinary (173), BuildFrameBin (181), UpdateFrameBin (193) all pass state. Process is declared `func (m *MockBackend) Process(_ unsafe.Pointer, input string)` (106) with the first parameter blank-discarded (it just delegates to processLocked(input) which never sees the pointer), so the divergence is purely cosmetic copy-paste drift with no behavioral effect.
suggested_fix: Pass state uniformly across all eight binary shims (or nil uniformly) so the mirror reads as deliberately consistent. Go-only (the mock is per-binding).

title: ExtractSignalsBin omits the defensive runtime.KeepAlive(data) that every sibling data-passing FFI method applies
proof: Re-read ffi.go. ExtractSignalsBin (782-835) sets `dataPtr = (*C.uint8_t)(unsafe.Pointer(&data[0]))` (799) and passes it to C.call_extract_signals_bin (806-816); the block immediately after the call (817-834) is the status-check + GoBytes path with NO runtime.KeepAlive(data). The siblings all have it: SendFrameBinary `runtime.KeepAlive(data)` at 505, ExtractSignalsBinary at 632, UpdateFrameBin at 763, and BuildFrameBin documents the rationale at 683-688 ("Defensive against future inliner / GC ... the GC could reclaim them while the C code is still reading. R19 cluster 12 - GO-B-10.3") with KeepAlive(indices/nums/dens/outBuf) at 689-692. ExtractSignalsBin is the lone data-passing method missing the established defensive pattern. Correctly NOT a live use-after-free: cgo's pointer-argument pinning rule keeps `data` alive for the duration of the call; this is copy-paste drift from the documented defensive convention.
suggested_fix: Add `runtime.KeepAlive(data)` immediately after the C.call_extract_signals_bin call (after line 816), matching SendFrameBinary/ExtractSignalsBinary/UpdateFrameBin. Go-only (cgo idiom).

title: extractCache comments reference a non-existent Client.mu lock field
proof: Re-read enrich.go and client.go. enrich.go line 296: "It is not thread-safe; all access must be synchronized by the caller (protected by Client.mu)." and line 303: "the Client holds Client.mu whenever it reads or writes entries." The Client struct (client.go 64-83) has fields backend/state/lockCh/closeOnce/closed/logger/defaultChecks/diags/cache/lastFrames/signalIndex/signalNames/lockWaiters — there is NO `mu` field. The serializing primitive is `lockCh chan struct{}` (client.go 67, a 1-deep channel semaphore, documented 51-56). `grep 'c\.mu|Client\.mu|sealedFormula|sealedPredicate'` over the package returns ONLY these two enrich.go comment lines plus the two from finding 6 — confirming `mu` exists nowhere in source.
suggested_fix: Replace the two "Client.mu" references with "the Client lock (lockCh)". Go-only comment defect.

title: Exhaustiveness comments cite non-existent sealedFormula / sealedPredicate markers
proof: Re-read enrich.go and ltl.go. enrich.go line 221 (default of collectSignalsInto): "Unreachable: the Formula interface is sealed (sealedFormula marker)"; line 247 (default of predicateSignal): "Unreachable: the Predicate interface is sealed (sealedPredicate marker)". The actual sealing methods are unexported `formula()` (ltl.go 140-153, e.g. `func (Atomic) formula() {}`) and `predicate()` (ltl.go 68-75, e.g. `func (Equals) predicate() {}`). grep for sealedFormula/sealedPredicate matches ONLY these two comment lines — the named markers do not exist in the source.
suggested_fix: Refer to the actual unexported marker methods formula() / predicate() instead of sealedFormula / sealedPredicate. Go-only comment defect.

title: Error-code group doc says "eight groups" but the parenthetical enumerates only seven names
proof: Re-read error.go. Lines 64-65: "Constants below are organized into eight groups (Parse / DBC text / Frame / Route / Handler / Dispatch / Extraction)" — the parenthetical names exactly 7 groups. The source has 8 distinct comment-header-separated blocks: Parse (71-107), DBC text (109-114), Frame (116-132), the standalone Input-bound block CodeInputBoundExceeded (134-140, separated by its own blank lines above and below), Route (142-159), Handler (161-178), Dispatch (180-187), Extraction (189-198). The count word "eight" is correct but the enumeration omits the Input-bound block — a self-contradiction within the same doc comment.
suggested_fix: Add "Input bound" to the parenthetical: (Parse / DBC text / Frame / Input bound / Route / Handler / Dispatch / Extraction). Go-only comment defect.

title: parseYAMLChecks cost comment uses Excel "per workbook" terminology in a YAML-bytes function
proof: Re-read yaml.go. Lines 154-156 (in parseYAMLChecks, which takes `data []byte` of YAML and only ever calls yaml.Unmarshal): "Cost is negligible on the loader path (microseconds per workbook); benefit is actionable diagnostics." "Workbook" is the Excel/excelize unit (go/excel/excel.go:80,111,135,162 use it correctly for actual .xlsx workbooks); it is wrong for a YAML document/file path. DROPPED the finder's provenance claim that this was "copied from the Excel loader's analogous comment" — verified there is NO twin two-pass/microseconds-per-workbook comment in go/excel/excel.go to have been copied from; the defect stands on the wrong-term-for-a-YAML-function basis alone.
suggested_fix: Replace "per workbook" with "per document" (or "per file"). Go-only comment defect.

title: CLI re-loads the DBC via ParseDBC after loadDBCText (ParseDBCText) already parsed and loaded it into the core
proof: Re-read main.go, client.go, and the Agda handlers. cmdExtract calls loadDBCText at 328 -> loadDBCText (170-187) calls client.ParseDBCText (182), which (client.go 328-367) sends the parseDBCText command and calls populateSignalLookup (361). The Agda handleParseDBCText (src/Aletheia/Protocol/Handlers/ParseDBCText.agda:131) transitions the core to `ReadyToStream 0 dbc [] emptyCache` — the DBC is now loaded in the core. Then main.go line 347 calls client.ParseDBC(def), which re-serializes def, makes a second parseDBC FFI round-trip, re-parses the response, and re-runs populateSignalLookup (client.go 313); handleParseDBC (Handlers.agda:227) reaches the SAME `ReadyToStream 0 dbc [] emptyCache`. The subsequent ExtractSignals (350) only needs the core in a DBC-loaded state: handleExtractAllSignals (Handlers.agda:403) uses withDBCContext, which only requires `getDBC state` to be `just` (withDBC = require NoDBC (getDBC state), Handlers.agda:90-99). So the second ParseDBC is redundant. cmdFormatDBC has the identical redundant ParseDBC at 464 before FormatDBC (467); handleFormatDBC (Handlers.agda:418) also only uses withDBCContext. Cost: an extra full DBC serialize + FFI round-trip + response parse + cache rebuild per CLI invocation. Confirmed off the hot path (one-shot CLI startup), hence low.
suggested_fix: Drop the redundant client.ParseDBC(def) at main.go 347 and 464; loadDBCText -> ParseDBCText already loads the DBC into the core and populates the lookup cache. CROSS-BINDING: verified against ALL peers. Python (the reference per cpp/src/cli/cli.cpp:7) loads ONCE — python/aletheia/cli.py _load_dbc (185-209) does a pure client-side dbc_to_json conversion (no core load), then _cmd_extract calls client.parse_dbc(dbc) exactly once (442). C++ does the SAME double-load as Go: cli.cpp load_dbc_text (116-134) calls parse_dbc_text (129, loads core), then cmd_extract calls parse_dbc again (301) and cmd_format_dbc calls parse_dbc again (375). So Go and C++ both DIVERGE from the Python reference by performing two core loads where one suffices; fix Go and C++ in lockstep (drop the second parse_dbc in cmd_extract/cmd_format_dbc). Rust has no CLI yet (Phase 6 host row) -> n/a there.

title: serializeDBC marshals the entire DBC twice on every DBC command (throwaway size-probe marshal + caller's real marshal)
proof: Re-verified at json.go:219-230. `probe, err := json.Marshal(out)` (line 219) is consumed only by `if size := uint64(len(probe)); size > MaxDBCTextBytes` (line 223) — the marshaled bytes are discarded and the function `return out, nil` (line 230) hands back the map. Every caller re-marshals the identical structure: serializeCommand at json.go:25 does `b, err := json.Marshal(m)` over an `m` that embeds the dbcMap from serializeDBC, and DBCDefinition.MarshalJSON at json.go:51-55 does `m, err := serializeDBC(d); ... return json.Marshal(m)`. ParseDBC (client.go:290), ValidateDBC (client.go:375), FormatDBCText (client.go:439) and json.Marshal(dbc) thus serialize the full DBC twice. The doubled full marshal is real and avoidable. DOWNGRADE to low for two reasons the original claim overstated: (1) this is on the one-shot CONTROL-PLANE DBC commands (ParseDBC/ValidateDBC/FormatDBCText/MarshalJSON), NOT the per-frame streaming hot path (which is the binary processFrameDirect path) — so it is not on a per-frame hot path; (2) the extra work is O(N) (one extra full serialization), not O(N^2): the claim's 'O(N^2)/51s for 1000 messages' is the Agda CORE parseDBC cost, not this marshal, so that attribution is a misread.
suggested_fix: Eliminate the throwaway marshal. Either have serializeDBC return the marshaled []byte so MarshalJSON and the command path reuse it instead of re-marshaling the map, or move the MaxDBCTextBytes length check into serializeCommand to run on len(b) of the bytes it already produces. The C++ peer (cpp/src/json_serialize.cpp:336/493) builds and dumps the DBC JSON tree exactly once with no size probe, confirming the single-marshal shape is feasible (its byte cap is only on the text-input path, client.cpp:184).

title: readTypedRows issues one excelize GetCellType call per non-empty cell after already reading every value via GetRows
proof: Re-verified at excel.go:247-285. Line 248 `grid, err := f.GetRows(sheet)` reads every cell's string value in one pass; line 263 `val := strings.TrimSpace(grid[r][c])` consumes them. Lines 267-271 then, per non-empty cell, call `coord, cerr := excelize.CoordinatesToCellName(c+1, r+1)` and `ct, cterr := f.GetCellType(sheet, coord)`. I read excelize v2.10.1 (the resolved version in GOMODCACHE): GetCellType (cell.go:84) calls getCellStringFunc (cell.go:1492-1547), which per call does `f.mu.Lock()` (1493) AND `ws.mu.Lock()` (1500) — two mutex lock/unlock pairs — plus `ws.mergeCellsParser(cell)` (1502), `CellNameToCoordinates` (1506), a `sort.Find` over ws.SheetData.Row (1520, O(log N)), and a linear `for colIdx := range rowData.C` scan to locate the cell (1533, O(M)). readTypedRows is invoked once per sheet by loadSimpleChecks (excel.go:317), loadWhenThenChecks (excel.go:432) and LoadDbc (excel.go:165) — three sheets per workbook. So per sheet: N*M GetCellType calls, each O(log N + M) with 2 mutex pairs, all to recover a single isText boolean whose underlying value was already in `grid`. GetRows returns [][]string (no type info), so the type cannot be recovered from the existing call without a second pass — but a single worksheet traversal (excelize Rows() streaming iterator whose Cell carries .T, or reading the row's []xlsxC slice once) would replace the N*M re-navigations.
suggested_fix: Read cell types in a single worksheet pass rather than per-cell GetCellType: use excelize's streaming Rows() iterator (Cells carry .T) or read each row's cell slice once and inspect .T directly, avoiding N*M re-navigations and 2*N*M mutex acquisitions. The Python peer sidesteps this entirely — excel_loader.py:331 `ws.iter_rows(values_only=True)` yields natively-typed values (str vs number) in one pass, so the text-vs-numeric distinction is free; the inefficiency is Go-specific.

title: extractSignalValues copies the full frame payload into a string cache key on every call, including cache hits (peer C++ avoids this on hits)
proof: Re-verified at client.go:1084-1085. `key := frameKey{idValue: id.Value(), isExtended: id.IsExtended(), dlc: dlc.Value(), data: string(data)}` then `result, ok := c.cache.get(key)`. frameKey.data is typed `string` (enrich.go:291: `data string // string([]byte) for comparable map key`). The `string(data)` conversion is computed unconditionally and STORED INTO A STRUCT FIELD before the resulting struct is used as the map key, so Go's allocation-free `m[string(b)]` map-index special case (which applies only to a conversion written directly in the index expression of a map access) does NOT apply here — a fresh allocation + full byte-copy of the payload happens on every extractSignalValues call, including the common cache-hit branch (1086-1090). The C++ peer at client.cpp:806-825 documents and implements the avoidance: it does the lookup with a NON-OWNING `detail::FrameKeyView lookup{... .data = data}` (a span, line 808-809) via a transparent comparator (client.hpp:260 'transparent comparator that enables heterogeneous lookup by FrameKeyView') and only constructs the owning `FramePayload(data.begin(), data.end())` copy on the miss/insert branch (line 824). So Go pays a copy Go's hot path does not need and C++ explicitly elides. Severity low is correct: CAN payloads are small (<=8 bytes CAN 2.0B, <=64 bytes CAN-FD), so each copy is tiny, but it is real, on the per-violation enrichment path (reached via enrichStreamingViolation), and demonstrably avoidable per the C++ reference.
suggested_fix: Avoid the unconditional payload copy on hits. Options: (a) short-circuit `if c.cache == nil` is already present; add a copy-free lookup path — e.g. key on a fixed-size array for the common <=8/<=64 byte case, or a hash+verify scheme; (b) at minimum, restructure so `string(data)` is only materialized on the miss branch before `c.cache.put`. Mirror the C++ FrameKeyView heterogeneous-lookup optimization (cpp/src/client.cpp:806-824) so the hot-path hit is copy-free, restoring cross-binding parity.

title: LazyIndex caches indices into a public mutable vector with no invalidation → stale cached index reaches unchecked vector::operator[] (UB)
proof:
Re-read all cited lines with my own eyes; every citation holds and I found a third instance the finder missed.

POPULATE-ONCE, NEVER-INVALIDATED CACHE: dbc.hpp:34-39 `ensure`: `if (cache_) return; cache_.emplace(); std::forward<Builder>(build)(*cache_);` — the map is built on first call and never rebuilt or cleared. The only header comment (dbc.hpp:24) claims `Public interface is const-safe: ensure() populates once, find() reads` — it documents const-safety, NOT staleness/invalidation, so there is no immutability contract.

INDEXED FIELDS ARE PUBLIC MUTABLE AGGREGATE MEMBERS: dbc.hpp:113 `std::vector<DbcSignal> signals;` and dbc.hpp:371 `std::vector<DbcMessage> messages;` — plain public fields, no accessor guards the mutation (grep of dbc.hpp confirms only `always_present_signals`/`multiplexed_signals` const helpers exist; no setter, no const-only exposure). The struct docstring at dbc.hpp:359-366 explicitly invites aggregate construction (“Construct it programmatically (messages/signals as aggregate init)”), and the cache fields are themselves public+mutable (dbc.hpp:130, 393-394).

UNCHECKED DEREF → UB: THREE sites use `operator[]` (not `.at()`) on a cached index: dbc.cpp:99 `return &signals[*idx];` (signal_by_name), dbc.cpp:122 `return &messages[*idx];` (message_by_id), dbc.cpp:135 `return &messages[*idx];` (message_by_name — NOT cited by the finder; same hazard). For `*idx >= vec.size()`, `vector::operator[]` is undefined behaviour (OOB read).

CONCRETE TRIGGER (no prior reference required — this defeats the obvious ‘standard pointer-invalidation’ refutation): `auto* a = msg.signal_by_name("X"); /* freezes name->idx cache */ msg.signals.clear(); auto* b = msg.signal_by_name("X"); /* a FRESH call, holding nothing, reads the frozen idx and dereferences signals[idx] OOB */`. The hazard is NOT that a previously-returned pointer dangles (that would be ordinary C++ aliasing); it is that a brand-new lookup silently consults a stale index and returns a wrong-or-OOB pointer. A correct linear-scan lookup (what the peers do) would reflect the live vector.

The design embeds an unenforced, undocumented ‘do not mutate signals/messages after first lookup’ precondition whose violation is UB rather than a recoverable error — not memory-safe by construction.
suggested_fix:
Read all four peers. Python (python/aletheia/dbc/_queries.py:107-136 message_by_id/message_by_name/signal_by_name) and Rust (rust/src/dbc.rs:1037-1058) do pure linear scans with NO cache — a fresh lookup always reflects the live list, so they are IMMUNE to staleness. Go (go/aletheia/dbc.go:84-89 buildSignalIndex, :196-211 SignalByName, :612-661 buildIndexes/MessageByID/MessageByName) DOES cache the same way C++ does and SHARES the frozen-index staleness design flaw: with a stale in-bounds idx it silently returns the WRONG element (`out := m.Signals[idx]` at dbc.go:199) — a correctness bug present in BOTH Go and C++; only the OUT-OF-BOUNDS consequence differs (Go's slice index is bounds-checked → defined panic; C++'s `operator[]` → UB). So: classify this finding as single-binding because the UB is C++-only, but a maintainer must NOT read ‘Go is safe’ and fix C++ alone — Go's silent-wrong-element staleness is a latent correctness bug to address separately.
C++ fix: (a) cheap+immediate — bound-check before every deref: `if (*idx >= signals.size()) return nullptr;` at dbc.cpp:99 and `if (*idx >= messages.size()) return nullptr;` at BOTH dbc.cpp:122 and dbc.cpp:135 (removes the UB even under misuse); and/or (b) robust — store the resolved key/pointer rather than a positional index, or expose signals/messages through accessors that invalidate the LazyIndex on mutation; and document that mutating signals/messages after a lookup invalidates cached lookups. Mirror the chosen contract in Go so the silent-wrong-element case is closed there too (or drop the Go cache and linear-scan like Python/Rust).

title: parse_extraction_bin returns an empty SUCCESS on every truncated binary buffer (no error path), diverging from Go+Python peers and contradicting its own callsite comments
proof:
VERIFIED by reading cpp/src/client.cpp:273-344, cpp/include/aletheia/error.hpp:150, go/aletheia/json.go:1103-1169, python/aletheia/client/_client_bin.py:95-188.

CORE DEFECT (confirmed, ironclad): parse_extraction_bin returns `Result<ExtractionResult>` where `Result<T> = std::expected<T, AletheiaError>` (error.hpp:150). On EVERY truncation/short-buffer condition it executes `return {};` — line 290 (`if (buf.size() < 6) return {};`), line 299 (`if (buf.size() < expected_size) return {};`), line 309 (values per-loop), line 322 (errors per-loop), line 336 (absent per-loop). For `std::expected<ExtractionResult, AletheiaError>`, `return {};` value-initializes the CONTAINED VALUE (ExtractionResult), producing a SUCCESSFUL, empty result (has_value()==true) — NOT an error. The upfront `expected_size` check at 298-299 means any truncated buffer returns empty success before the loops even run. The public caller extract_signals returns it directly: line 362-363 `if (buf) return parse_extraction_bin(*buf, ...)`. So a truncated FFI response is silently presented as a frame that decoded with zero signals/errors/absent — indistinguishable from a legitimate empty extraction.

INTERNAL INCONSISTENCY (aggravator, confirmed): the SAME function at line 314-316 returns `std::unexpected(sv.error())` on a bad rational. The function demonstrably knows how to return an error in std::expected form; it deliberately chose `{}` (success) for truncation. This converts a possibly-intentional reading into a within-one-function inconsistency: invalid rational => error, but truncated buffer => silent success.

LYING COMMENTS (confirmed): comment at 353-356 states 'any other error (decode / truncation / real FFI failure) propagates, matching Go's ErrBinaryPathUnsupported contract.' Comment at 870-873 repeats 'any other error is a real failure (decode / truncation / genuine FFI error) logged + surfaced as std::nullopt.' Both claim truncation propagates as an error; the code swallows truncation into an empty success, so the error path at 880-886 (extract_signals_internal) is never reached for truncation and the std::unexpected propagation at 364-365 is never reached for truncation. The comments describe behavior the body does not deliver.

PEERS: Go (go/aletheia/json.go:1103-1169) returns protocolError on every short/truncated condition (1105 'too short', 1120 'truncated in values', 1136 'truncated in errors', 1153 'truncated in absent'). Python (python/aletheia/client/_client_bin.py) raises ProtocolError on every short/truncated condition (180-182 header too short, 111-113 truncated values, 134-136 truncated errors, 160-162 truncated absent). C++ is the lone outlier among the three bindings with this packed-binary path: it loses data silently instead of erroring.

SEVERITY DOWNGRADE rationale: the bounds checks DO prevent OOB reads (no memory unsafety — the bug is purely a wrong return VALUE, not UB). The trigger is an abnormal FFI state (version skew / a real .so bug / memory corruption) — a correctly-built .so emitting a well-formed buffer for valid caller input never reaches a truncation branch. This is a defense-in-depth gap at the FFI trust boundary plus a lying comment plus a parity divergence — medium, not high. I could not identify any valid-input path that produces a short buffer, which would have justified high.

TRAILING-BYTE SUB-CLAIM RE-FRAMED (the finder mis-scoped this): only Go has a trailing-byte check (go/aletheia/json.go:1163-1165). Python has NO trailing-byte check (verified: grep of _client_bin.py shows only `len(buf) < needed` short checks, no `off != len(buf)`). So on trailing bytes C++ is NOT the lone outlier — C++ and Python both lack it; this is a Go-only enhancement, NOT part of the confirmed C++-outlier divergence. It is an additive, weaker item, scoped out of the core finding.
suggested_fix: Replace each `return {};` in parse_extraction_bin (cpp/src/client.cpp:290, 299, 309, 322, 336) with `return std::unexpected(AletheiaError{ErrorKind::Protocol, ...})`, message-matching the Go/Python peers ('binary extraction buffer too short', 'truncated in values/errors/absent'). This brings C++ into lockstep with Go+Python (the parity fix) and makes the 353-356 / 870-873 comments truthful. SEPARATELY and ADDITIVELY (not part of the C++-outlier defect): a final trailing-byte check (`if (off != buf.size()) return protocol error`) would match go/aletheia/json.go:1163-1165 — but note this would also need adding to Python to reach full parity, since Python currently lacks it too; treat trailing-byte rejection as its own lockstep item across C+++Python, gated on whether the project wants Go's stricter contract everywhere.

title: Rational comment + static_assert message claim g++/clang-21 support that the build hard-rejects
proof: Re-read with my own eyes. types.hpp:105-106 comment: "// __int128 is a GCC/Clang extension (not standard C++); the project targets // g++ >= 14 and clang >= 21 on Linux only, where it is always available." and types.hpp:109-110 static_assert message: "Aletheia requires __int128 support " "(g++ >= 14 / clang >= 21 on Linux).". This is contradicted by the toolchain SSOT and the build itself: cpp/CMakeLists.txt:14-18 `if(NOT CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER_ID STREQUAL "Clang")` fatals "aletheia-cpp is Clang-only (got ...); g++ is not supported", and CMakeLists.txt:19-23 `elseif(CMAKE_CXX_COMPILER_VERSION VERSION_LESS 22)` warns the supported floor is Clang 22. docs/development/BUILDING.md (toolchain-support-policy SSOT, verified lines 42-50) states "currently **Clang 22**" and "g++ is not supported for the C++ binding". The peer FFI source proves types.hpp is the stale outlier within the same binding: ffi_backend.cpp:84-86 correctly says "std::uint8_t is unsigned char on every platform we target (Clang 22 on Linux x86_64/ARM64)". The comment names the wrong supported toolchain (g++ at all; Clang 21 vs 22). DOWNGRADED to low: unlike finding 4 this is a pure-prose lie with zero behavioral impact — the build self-corrects (CMakeLists fatals on g++) and the static_assert never fires on a supported toolchain (sizeof(__int128) >= 16 holds on Clang 22). Keeping it medium would flatten the scale against the real missing-feature parity bug (finding 4).
suggested_fix: Replace both the comment and the static_assert message with the supported toolchain: "the project targets the latest stable Clang (currently 22) on Linux only" — drop the g++ reference and bump 21 to 22 to match CMakeLists.txt and BUILDING.md.

title: ErrorCode doc says "51 codes across 6 families"; actually 57 codes (56 sub-codec + 1 standalone) across 7 families
proof: Re-read and counted independently. error.hpp:39-40: "Each named constant other than `Unknown` maps 1:1 to a string emitted by `errorCode` in `Aletheia.Error` — the ADT defines 51 codes across 6 families (Parse / Frame / Route / Handler / Dispatch / Extraction)." Ground truth, three converging counts: (1) the enum itself has exactly 57 constants excluding Unknown (awk over the enum body: 57). (2) Agda emits 57 distinct code strings — the seven sub-codec functions (parseErrorCode, frameErrorCode, extractionErrorCode, routeErrorCode, handlerErrorCode, dispatchErrorCode, dbcTextParseErrorCode) account for 56 distinct strings, PLUS `InputBoundExceeded` which is handled inline in `errorCode` (src/Aletheia/Error.agda:429 `errorCode (InputBoundExceeded _ _ _) = "input_bound_exceeded"`) and belongs to none of those families: 56 + 1 = 57. (3) cpp/src/json_parse.cpp's lookup table holds exactly 57 string→code initializers. So the count is 57 not 51. The family count is also wrong: `errorCode` (src/Aletheia/Error.agda:420-429) dispatches over SEVEN sub-codecs, and the C++ surface itself proves the omitted seventh — error.hpp:69-72 declares the "DBC text parse errors" block (DBCTextParseFailure / DBCTextTrailingInput / DBCTextAttributeRefinementFailed) that the comment's 6-family list drops. Verdict confirmed; severity stays low (doc-count drift, no behavioral effect).
suggested_fix: Update to "the ADT defines 57 codes across 7 sub-codec families (Parse / DBC text parse / Frame / Route / Handler / Dispatch / Extraction); the seven families account for 56, plus the standalone `input_bound_exceeded`." The "51 codes" count is also stale in the Go peer (go/aletheia/error.go:61 "51 codes") — per the cross-binding consistency rule, fix both bindings; cite a formula (std::size(error_code_table)) instead of a literal so it cannot drift again. The 6-families/DBC-text omission is C++-only — Go's comment does not enumerate families.

title: signal_index_/signal_names_ comments say "Populated by parse_dbc()" but parse_dbc_text() populates them too — self-contradicted 30 lines up
proof: Re-read with my own eyes. client.hpp:274-275: "// Signal name → 0-based index within the DBC message's signal list. // Populated by parse_dbc(); cleared by parse_dbc()." and client.hpp:278-279: "// Index → signal name reverse lookup, keyed by (can_id_value, is_extended). // Populated alongside signal_index_ in parse_dbc()." Contradicted within the SAME header at client.hpp:246-248: "// Refresh signal name → index cache from a parsed DBC. Shared between // parse_dbc() and parse_dbc_text(); both paths land here on success." and by the implementation: populate_signal_lookup() (cpp/src/client.cpp:139-156) clears (lines 140-141 `signal_index_.clear(); signal_names_.clear();`) and writes BOTH maps, and is invoked from BOTH parse_dbc (client.cpp:166) AND parse_dbc_text (client.cpp:200). The "cleared by parse_dbc()" wording is also misleading — the clear happens in populate_signal_lookup on either parse path. Note: the implementing class is named AletheiaClient (the finder wrote "Client"); this naming detail does not affect the proof — the cited line numbers and bodies are exactly as stated.
suggested_fix: Reword both member comments to "Populated/cleared by populate_signal_lookup(), i.e. on every parse_dbc() and parse_dbc_text() success", matching the sibling comment at client.hpp:246-248.

title: create_excel_template doc claims "column formatting" and "headers in bold"; C++ writes plain cells while Python AND Go peers both bold them
proof: Re-read with my own eyes. excel.hpp:39-42: "/// Create a blank Excel template with headers and column formatting. /// /// Writes a .xlsx file with three sheets (DBC, Checks, When-Then), /// each with correct headers in bold. Does not overwrite existing files." The implementation does NEITHER: create_excel_template (cpp/src/excel.cpp:660-695) only calls write_header_row for each sheet, and write_header_row (cpp/src/excel.cpp:469-472) is `for (...) ws.cell(1, i+1).value() = headers[i];` — plain string assignment, no font/bold/style. grep -niE 'bold|font|format|style' over cpp/src/excel.cpp returns only two hits, both inside unrelated error-message strings ("format the cell as a number" at lines 164, 182). This is the strongest of the six: it is also a PARITY DIVERGENCE, not just a doc lie. Both peers that ship the template actually bold the headers — Python excel_loader.py:394-399 (`bold = Font(bold=True)` then `cell.font = bold`) and Go go/excel/excel.go:199 (`f.NewStyle(&excelize.Style{Font: &excelize.Font{Bold: true}})`) applied at go/excel/excel.go:296 (`f.SetCellStyle(...)`). C++ is the sole binding that silently drops the documented bold-header behavior. Medium is justified: a real missing-feature gap, not a doc nit.
suggested_fix: Implement bold-header formatting via OpenXLSX cell formats so C++ matches Python (excel_loader.py:396-399) and Go (go/excel/excel.go:199,296). If bold is genuinely out of scope for C++, instead correct the docstring to "each with the correct header row" (drop "column formatting" and "in bold") AND flip the corresponding FEATURE_MATRIX expectation — but parity with the peers is the preferred fix.

title: last_frames_ comment says "cleared by start_stream()" but end_stream() also clears it
proof: Re-read with my own eyes. client.hpp:264-266: "// Last frame seen per CAN ID, for end-of-stream enrichment. // Populated by send_frame() (guarded by !diags_.empty()); cleared by start_stream(). // Storage: one entry per unique (CAN ID, extended) pair." In fact last_frames_ is cleared in TWO places, verified by reading both call sites: start_stream (cpp/src/client.cpp:516 `last_frames_.clear();`, inside the has_value() success block) and end_stream (cpp/src/client.cpp:654 `last_frames_.clear();`, after the enrichment loop at lines 646-651 runs). A reader trusting the comment would believe the last-frame map survives an end_stream() until the next start_stream(), which is false — it is wiped at the end of every end_stream() success path.
suggested_fix: Reword to "cleared by start_stream() and at the end of end_stream() (after enrichment)."

title: error_code_table over-declared as std::array<...,59> with 57 initializers — 2 silent value-initialized {"", Unknown} padding entries
proof: Re-read and counted independently. json_parse.cpp:33 `constexpr std::array<ErrorCodeEntry, 59> error_code_table{{` with the comment at lines 30-31 "Linear scan is fine // for the 59-entry table". The brace list contains exactly 57 `{"...", ErrorCode::...}` initializers (awk count over lines 34-99 = 57; last entry extraction_bit_extraction_failed at line 98, closing `}};` at line 99). Declared size 59 minus 57 initializers = 2 value-initialized trailing elements. ErrorCodeEntry is std::pair<std::string_view, ErrorCode> (line 32); value-init makes them {std::string_view{}, ErrorCode{}} = {"", ErrorCode::Unknown} since Unknown is the first enumerator (error.hpp:48) hence value 0. They are inert to lookups: error_code_from_string (json_parse.cpp:103-108) returns Unknown on no-match anyway, so matching "" → Unknown changes nothing for any real input. Confirmed as dead padding + the source of the cross-surface count confusion (error.hpp says 51, this array says 59, the real total is 57). Severity stays low — genuinely inert, just sloppy and drift-prone.
suggested_fix: Drop the explicit size so it cannot drift: `constexpr std::array error_code_table = std::to_array<ErrorCodeEntry>({ ... });` (CTAD via std::to_array), and reword the comment to derive the count from std::size(error_code_table) instead of the literal "59".

title: ExtractionResult.errors is an anonymous std::pair — sole binding without a named SignalError type
proof: Re-read cpp/include/aletheia/response.hpp:41 myself: `std::vector<std::pair<SignalName, std::string>> errors;` inside the public `struct ExtractionResult` (returned from extract_signals). The `.first`/`.second` carry no field meaning. I re-read all three peers and every one defines a NAMED struct: go/aletheia/result.go:13-16 `type SignalError struct { Name SignalName; Error string }` (ExtractionResult.Errors is `[]SignalError` at result.go:23); rust/src/response.rs:95-100 `pub struct SignalError { pub name: String, pub reason: String }` (ExtractionResult.errors is `Vec<SignalError>` at response.rs:108, and the rustdoc at response.rs:91-93 explicitly says it 'Mirrors the Go `SignalError` / Python `errors`'); python/aletheia/types.py:460-471 `class SignalError(TypedDict): name: str; error: str`. So C++ is the lone outlier using a positional anonymous pair while all three peers use a named field type — a parity divergence under AGENTS.md:31 (a cross-binding finding is about the whole feature; here peers are consistent and C++ diverges → disposition (a) parity bug, fix C++ to the named type). Spec citation chain holds exactly.
suggested_fix: Introduce `struct SignalError { SignalName name; std::string error; };` in response.hpp and change `ExtractionResult.errors` to `std::vector<SignalError>` (use field name `error` to match Go/Python; Rust's `reason` is itself a minor peer divergence). Update the emplace sites in json_parse.cpp and client.cpp. This is a BREAKING public-header change — add a CHANGELOG.md entry per AGENTS.md:48.

title: Dead function: format_value(double) overload has zero callers
proof: Re-read cpp/src/enrich.cpp:20-22: `static auto format_value(double v) -> std::string { return std::format("{:g}", v); }`. I ran `rg -n "format_value" cpp/src cpp/include`: the only call sites are enrich.cpp lines 54,57,60,63,66,68,69,76,78,81. I read each — every argument is `v.value.get()` / `v.min.get()` / `v.max.get()` / `v.delta.get()` / `v.tolerance.get()`, all of which return `Rational` (the predicate value newtypes wrap Rational), binding to the `format_value(const Rational&)` overload at enrich.cpp:32. No call passes a `double`. The double overload at :20-22 is therefore dead. Violates AGENTS/cpp.md:15 cat 4 'no unused types, functions, or includes'.
suggested_fix: Delete the unused `format_value(double)` overload at enrich.cpp:20-22.

title: Dead branch: `if (den < 0)` in Rational::from_double is unreachable
proof: Re-read cpp/src/types.cpp myself: :36 `constexpr std::int64_t scale = 1'000'000'000;` (positive constant), :55 `auto g = std::gcd(std::abs(num), scale);` (gcd of a non-negative and a positive ⇒ g >= 1), :56 `auto den = scale / g;` ⇒ den is positive/non-negative integer division of two positives, always >= 1. Therefore the branch :58-61 `if (den < 0) { num = -num; den = -den; }` is unreachable. The team's own parallel precedent confirms the reasoning: I read cpp/src/json_serialize.cpp:44-49, which documents removing the identically-unreachable `den < 0` normalization ('The den < 0 normalization here was unreachable dead code ... Removed — it harbored untestable surviving mutants under Mull'). The same argument applies verbatim here but the branch was left in. Cat 4 dead code (cpp.md:15).
suggested_fix: Remove the `if (den < 0)` block at types.cpp:58-61 (den is provably positive). If a defensive note is wanted, mirror the json_serialize.cpp:44-49 comment instead of keeping a branch that survives mutation testing.

title: Copy-paste duplicated severity-parse + issue-build loop in parse_validation and parse_parsed_dbc
proof: Re-read both blocks. parse_validation, json_parse.cpp:736-750: `auto sev_str = issue.value("severity", ""); IssueSeverity severity{}; if (sev_str == "error") {severity = IssueSeverity::Error;} else if (sev_str == "warning") {severity = IssueSeverity::Warning;} else {return std::unexpected(make_error(ErrorKind::Protocol, "Unknown validation severity: " + sev_str));} issues.push_back({.severity = severity, .code = parse_issue_code(issue.value("code", "")), .detail = issue.value("detail", "")});`. parse_parsed_dbc, json_parse.cpp:977-991: byte-for-byte identical logic, the only difference being it pushes into `warnings` instead of `issues`. The cited DRY convention in the same file is real — `rg` confirms helpers parse_property_result_entry at :818 (called :885, :929) and parse_stream_warning_entry at :902 (called :934), so per-entry decode is already factored elsewhere; this issue-decode loop was not. Low-quality copy-paste-drift risk confirmed.
suggested_fix: Extract `static auto parse_issue_entry(const Json& issue) -> Result<ValidationIssue>` (Protocol error on unknown severity) and call it from both loops, matching the existing parse_*_entry helper convention in the same file.

title: LazyIndex caches under const via unsynchronized mutable state; 'const-safe' comment masks a concurrent-read data race
proof: Re-read cpp/include/aletheia/dbc.hpp:34-39 `template<typename Builder> void ensure(Builder&& build) const { if (cache_) return; cache_.emplace(); std::forward<Builder>(build)(*cache_); }` with :50 `mutable std::optional<std::unordered_map<...>> cache_;` — no mutex/atomic/once_flag. The class comment at dbc.hpp:24 states 'Public interface is const-safe: ensure() populates once, find() reads.' This backs the `const` public lookups DbcDefinition::message_by_id / message_by_name (caches at dbc.hpp:393-394, mutable) and DbcMessage::signal_by_name (cache at dbc.hpp:130, mutable). I confirmed DbcDefinition is a plain value type with four obtain-paths incl. programmatic construction (dbc.hpp:358-365) — NOT the Client. I read the only thread-safety doc (client.hpp:41-48); it scopes single-thread-only to AletheiaClient and says 'no shared mutable state between instances' — it does NOT cover DbcDefinition, and `rg -ni 'thread|concurren'` over dbc.hpp returns nothing. So a caller sharing a `const DbcDefinition&` read-only across two threads that both call message_by_id before the cache is built races on cache_.emplace() + the builder write — UB ([res.on.data.races] makes 'const ⇒ concurrent-read-safe' the standard expectation a reader applies). The 'const-safe' comment actively invites this. Same pattern (also unsynchronized) at check.hpp:96-102 condition_desc() writing mutable condition_desc_/condition_desc_builder_ under const. The safe pattern is known in-tree: rational_renderer.cpp uses std::once_flag/call_once (lines 40,120,186). Severity medium upheld: the misleading comment compounds the trap, and the trigger (shared read-only const DBC) is a natural usage the docs neither bless nor forbid.
suggested_fix: Either (a) reword dbc.hpp:24 from 'const-safe' to 'const-correct, NOT thread-safe' and document that these const lookups are not safe for concurrent first-call on a shared instance (matching how client.hpp scopes the Client); or (b) make the lazy population genuinely race-free via std::once_flag/std::call_once inside LazyIndex (the pattern rational_renderer.cpp already uses) so const truly implies concurrent-read-safe. Apply the same to check.hpp:96-102.

title: Enriched reason mixes exact FFI-rendered formula text with a lossy double-rendered signal value
proof: Re-read cpp/src/client.cpp:713-714 `reason += std::format("{} = {:g}", std::string_view{sig}, it->second.get().to_double());` — the per-signal observed value is rendered via to_double() (lossy ℚ→double) + {:g}. The same reason string then appends the formula half at client.cpp:721 `reason += " (formula: " + diag.formula_desc + ")";`, where diag.formula_desc was built in enrich.cpp via format_value(const Rational&) → format_rational_ffi, the exact Agda-kernel renderer whose own comment (enrich.cpp:24-31) insists output is byte-identical 'by construction ... no local C++ fallback'. So one human-readable string renders the same Rational value type two ways: exact for thresholds inside the formula, approximate for observed values (a 1/3 signal prints '0.333333'). I verified peers per AGENTS.md:31: go/aletheia/enrich.go:263 `formatValue(float64(val))` and python/aletheia/client/_enrichment.py:410 `f"{sig} = {val:g}"` — both render the live value lossily via float while their formula descs use the exact rational renderer. Consistent across all three bindings ⇒ disposition (c) consistent-but-arguably-against-the-stated-exact-render-principle: a maintainer technical-direction call, not a C++-only divergence. Confirmed as such; not unilaterally 'fix all'.
suggested_fix: Surface to maintainer (AGENTS.md:31 direction call): either accept that observed values in a human-readable reason are intentionally approximate (then document, in all three enrichment files, that the exact-render guarantee covers only thresholds/formula text), or render observed values through the same format_rational_ffi exact path. Whichever is chosen, apply it to all three bindings in lockstep, not C++ alone.

title: end_stream re-extracts every last-known frame once per failing property (P×F FFI crossings for at most F distinct extractions)
proof: Re-verified at the cited lines. AletheiaClient::end_stream (client.cpp:646-651) loops over every Fails/Unresolved PropertyResult and calls enrich_property_result(pr). enrich_property_result (client.cpp:792) calls extract_last_known_values(diag). extract_last_known_values (client.cpp:846-863) iterates all of last_frames_ and calls extract_signals_internal(last_frame.id, last_frame.dlc, last_frame.data, key.first, key.second) per frame. extract_signals_internal (client.cpp:867-925) issues a real FFI call every time — line 877 backend_->extract_signals_bin or line 908 backend_->extract_signals_binary — and, unlike the hot-path extract_signal_values (client.cpp:802-838) which consults cache_, it bypasses the cache entirely. The extraction inputs come only from last_frames_ and the key, never from diag, so the per-frame ExtractionResult is property-independent and reusable across all failing properties. There is no memoization across the per-property calls. The loop has a wanted.empty() early-break (client.cpp:861-862) so each per-property call extracts at most F frames, making the finder's 'up to P×F' an accurate upper bound (worst case: each property's signals are only satisfied by the last frame examined). The redundant work scales with the count of failing properties, not with new data. Severity low is correct: this is the EOS finalization path, not the per-frame streaming hot path.
suggested_fix: Hoist the per-frame extraction once in end_stream (or in a new extract_last_known_values overload) before the property loop: build a single merged map of (signal -> value) by extracting each entry of last_frames_ exactly once, then filter that pre-computed map per diag. This is exactly what the Rust binding already does (rust/src/lib.rs:435-488 enrich_eos): it extracts each distinct frame once into a `merged` vector (loop at lib.rs:456-467), THEN iterates failing properties and filters merged per diagnostic (lib.rs:468-473) — reducing P×F crossings to F. The finder labeled this consistent-wrong after reading Python/Go/C++; I additionally read the 4th peer (Rust), which DIVERGES by doing it correctly. Rust's extract_all doc-comment (lib.rs:325-327) makes the once-per-distinct-frame contract explicit. Correct cross-binding label is therefore 'diverges': Rust is the reference; C++ and Go perform genuinely uncached P×F FFI crossings (verified extract_signals_internal / extractSignalsLocked both bypass any extraction cache — go/aletheia/client.go:1133-1177), and Python re-iterates per property but its extract_signals path is cached (python/aletheia/client/_streaming.py:588-630), so its magnitude is milder though the per-P redundancy still exists. C++/Go/Python should adopt Rust's hoist-then-filter shape.

title: load_dbc_from_excel deep-copies each row's CellMap into data_rows instead of moving the dead local
proof: Re-verified excel.cpp:627-633. `auto cells = row_to_map(ws, ...)` (line 628) builds a fresh local CellMap; line 631 `data_rows.push_back(cells)` copies it by lvalue. After line 631 the only remaining statement is `row_numbers.push_back(...)` (line 632), which does not touch `cells`, and the loop re-binds `cells` via `auto cells = ...` next iteration — so `cells` is provably dead after the push_back. CellMap = std::map<std::string, CellVal> (excel.cpp:109) and CellVal { std::string value; bool is_text; } (excel.cpp:103-106), so each copied node deep-copies the key string AND the value string — two heap allocations per cell, all avoidable. The contrast is accurate: load_checks_from_excel (excel.cpp:508-525) consumes its CellMap in place via parse_simple_row(cells, ...) / parse_when_then_row(cells, ...) which take `const CellMap&` (excel.cpp:274, 330) and never store a copy. The standard move-the-dead-local idiom applies.
suggested_fix: Use data_rows.push_back(std::move(cells)); cells is dead after the push. This is a C++ value-semantics micro-optimization with no peer analogue (Python/Go append references, not deep copies), correctly classified single-binding. Only on the file-load path, not streaming, so low severity is correct.

title: load_dbc_from_excel re-looks-up each message group via groups[key] though group_rows_by_message already returns the populated map
proof: Re-verified excel.cpp:638-646. group_rows_by_message (excel.cpp:545-565) returns a std::map<MessageKeyExt, std::vector<std::size_t>> already populated (line 562 groups[key].push_back(i)) plus an insertion_order vector. The subsequent loop (excel.cpp:641-646) iterates insertion_order and re-fetches each vector with groups[key] (line 642), re-running the O(log n) ordered-map lookup over MessageKeyExt = tuple<uint32_t, std::string, int64_t, bool> (excel.cpp:541) — a tuple+string comparison per message — that group_rows_by_message already performed. Every insertion_order key was inserted into groups (lines 560-562), so the lookup is guaranteed to hit and is purely redundant. Note groups[key] uses non-const std::map::operator[] (groups is a local from a structured binding, line 638), which is correct here but is the redundant-lookup form. Severity low: O(M log M) redundant lookups during file load only, not streaming.
suggested_fix: Carry the grouped index-vectors in insertion order — e.g. return std::vector<std::pair<MessageKeyExt, std::vector<std::size_t>>> — or iterate groups directly while tracking first-seen order, so build_message_from_group receives the vector without a second groups[key] lookup. C++ container-access idiom with no peer analogue (Python/Go map iteration differs), correctly single-binding.

title: structured_logging matrix note overstates Rust's emission: claims it 'emits the canonical event vocabulary' but it emits only 11 of 16 events
proof: Re-read FEATURE_MATRIX.yaml:432: notes string ends '...Emits the canonical event vocabulary (docs/LOG_EVENTS.yaml), validated by the rust/tests/log_events.rs parity gate.' This is overstated. `grep -rnoE 'events::[A-Z_]+' rust/src/*.rs` excluding log.rs definitions yields exactly 11 distinct EMIT sites (backend.rs:267 RTS_CORES_MISMATCH; lib.rs:359 ENRICHMENT_PROPERTY_INDEX_OOB; 406/479 ENRICHMENT_EXTRACTION_FAILED; 520/558 DBC_PARSED; 614 PROPERTIES_SET; 652 STREAM_STARTED; 696 FRAME_PROCESSED; 711 ERROR_EVENT_SENT; 726 REMOTE_EVENT_SENT; 744 STREAM_ENDED; 754 ENDSTREAM_UNCACHED_ATOM).
