The other 5 (CACHE_HIT/MISS/FULL at log.rs:156-160, EXTRACTION_PROCESS_FAILED at log.rs:162, EXTRACTION_PARSE_FAILED at log.rs:164) sit under the honest header 'Vocabulary completeness (not yet emitted by this client)' (log.rs:146) and the events module doc-comment (log.rs:120-123) explicitly says 'so downstream code can refer to any vocabulary event uniformly... even where this client does not yet emit it'. The gate is NOT what the note implies: log_events.rs:38-52 hard-codes the 11-event `rust_emitted()` list; the only emission test (lines 80-92) asserts each emitted event is IN the vocabulary (emitted ⊆ vocabulary), never that all 16 are emitted; the second test (64-77) checks the `events::ALL` constant array equals the YAML names (a definition bijection, not emission). I verified peers emit all 16: Python emits EXTRACTION_PROCESS_FAILED/PARSE_FAILED (_signal_ops.py:145,158) and cache.* (_streaming.py:154,171,191); Go emits all (client.go:1088-1172); C++ emits all (client.cpp:814-919). The note is correct for Go/Python/C++ but false for Rust. So 'Emits the canonical event vocabulary' is a lying/overstated FEATURE_MATRIX claim that log.rs itself contradicts.
suggested_fix: Rewrite the FEATURE_MATRIX structured_logging rust note to match what log.rs already documents per-constant: Rust emits 11 of the canonical 16 events; the 3 cache.* events belong to an unimplemented extraction-cache layer, and the 2 extraction.{process,parse}_failed events are not yet wired (see Finding B). State plainly that the rust/tests/log_events.rs gate validates `emitted ⊆ vocabulary` (subset) plus the `events::ALL` definition bijection, NOT full emission. Reword 'Emits the canonical event vocabulary' to e.g. 'Defines the full canonical event vocabulary (events::ALL); emits 11 of 16 today.'

title: Rust never emits extraction.process_failed / extraction.parse_failed even though its enrichment-extraction path hits both failure modes (peers emit them)
proof: Re-read lib.rs:328-336: `extract_all` calls `self.extract_signals(id, dlc, data).ok()?` (line 329) — `.ok()` converts Result to Option, DISCARDING the Err before `?`, so neither failure mode is logged here. Re-read extract_signals (lib.rs:771-780): line 778 `let raw = self.backend.extract_signals_binary(id, dlc, data)?;` is the FFI/process step (its Err is the process-failure case) and line 779 `response::decode_extraction(&raw)` is the parse step (its Err is the parse-failure case); both surface as Result::Err and both are swallowed by the `.ok()?` at line 329. Grep confirms EXTRACTION_PROCESS_FAILED and EXTRACTION_PARSE_FAILED have ZERO emit sites in rust/src (the only emit-site grep results are the 11-event set; both extraction-error constants appear only as definitions at log.rs:162/164 and in events::ALL). The only enrichment-failure event Rust emits is the generic ENRICHMENT_EXTRACTION_FAILED (lib.rs:406 in diag_values, lib.rs:479 in enrich_eos) — collapsing the process-vs-parse distinction. Peer reference verified directly: Go go/aletheia/client.go extractSignalsLocked emits 'extraction.parse_failed' (lines 1142, 1172) on parse error and 'extraction.process_failed' (lines 1151, 1164) on FFI error, kept distinct from the higher-level 'enrichment.extraction_failed' (line 1106); Python _signal_ops.py:145/158 and C++ client.cpp:882/891/911/919 do the same on the same extraction path. The cache.* events are correctly excluded by FEATURE_MATRIX.yaml:456 ('the cache.* / extraction-cache layer is not implemented'), but that note covers only the cache layer — extraction.{process,parse}_failed live in LOG_EVENTS.yaml's separate 'Extraction errors (warn)' domain (lines 98-106) and are NOT a documented non-goal. AGENTS.md cross-binding parity ('same surface across bindings; divergences are bugs') makes this an exercised-path divergence, not a non-goal. Confirmed as a real, lower-severity (diagnostic-granularity) gap.
suggested_fix: In extract_signals (or a thin wrapper feeding extract_all), match on the two error sites instead of `?`-propagating opaquely: on the `self.backend.extract_signals_binary(...)` Err emit events::EXTRACTION_PROCESS_FAILED, and on the `response::decode_extraction(...)` Err emit events::EXTRACTION_PARSE_FAILED (both carrying canId + error detail to match Go's slog fields) before returning the Err. Keep ENRICHMENT_EXTRACTION_FAILED as the higher-level enrichment-skipped signal. Then add the two events to rust/tests/log_events.rs `rust_emitted()` so the parity gate enforces them.

title: Doc falsely claims `std::sync::mpsc::Sender` is `!Sync` (the stated reason for the `Mutex`)
proof: Re-read all three comment sites verbatim and they match the claim exactly. (1) async_client.rs:13-14 module doc: "(`mpsc::Sender` is `Send` but `!Sync`, hence the `Mutex` — held only for the brief enqueue, never across an `.await`)." (2) async_client.rs:47-49 field doc on `jobs`: "wrapped in a `Mutex` so `AsyncClient` is `Sync` (the std `mpsc::Sender` is `Send` but `!Sync`)." (3) async_client.rs:426-427 test comment in `async_client_is_send_and_sync`: "The std mpsc::Sender is !Sync, which is why `jobs` is Mutex-wrapped". The field is `jobs: Mutex<Option<Sender<Job>>>` (line 51), `type Job = Box<dyn FnOnce(&Client) + Send>` (line 43). EMPIRICAL DISPROOF on the in-repo toolchain (verified `rustc --version` = rustc 1.96.0; rust/Cargo.toml sets only `edition = "2021"`, NO `rust-version`/MSRV pin, so latest-toolchain-only policy applies): compiled `fn is_sync<T: Sync>(){} ... is_sync::<std::sync::mpsc::Sender<i32>>(); is_sync::<Sender<Box<dyn FnOnce()+Send>>>();` → EXIT 0. Therefore `Sender<T>: Sync` for `T: Send`, and since `Job: Send`, `Sender<Job>: Sync` — the `!Sync` claim is factually false (true since the Rust 1.72 mpsc rewrite). SECONDARY claim also verified: compiled a struct mirroring `AsyncClient` WITHOUT the Mutex (`jobs: Option<Sender<Job>>`, `worker: Option<JoinHandle<()>>`) and `is_sync::<that>()` → EXIT 0, so the `Mutex` is NOT what makes `AsyncClient: Sync` — the "hence the Mutex" rationale is false. The two `#[test]`s (`async_client_is_send_and_sync`, `method_futures_are_send`, lines 421-458) only assert `AsyncClient: Send + Sync` and that method futures are `Send`; both hold with or without the `Mutex` precisely because `Sender: Sync`, so they pass while the comment's `!Sync` premise is false — they do not validate the comment. The `Drop` impl (lines 398-414) uses `self.jobs.get_mut()` under `&mut self`, which works on the raw field too, so removing the `Mutex` would not break the close-then-join handshake either.
suggested_fix: Correct the false `!Sync` claim at all three sites (async_client.rs:13-14, 47-49, 426-427): on the supported toolchain `std::sync::mpsc::Sender<T>` is `Sync` for `T: Send`. If the `Mutex` is retained, give its real justification (e.g. centralising the `take()`-to-close handshake or guarding the `Option` swap) rather than the disproven `Sync` rationale. Separately (an ill-designed/efficiency follow-up, not part of fixing the lie): since `Sender<Job>: Sync` and `Option`/`JoinHandle` are `Sync`, the `Mutex` is not required for `AsyncClient: Sync`; `Sender::send` takes `&self` so enqueue needs no lock and `Drop` already holds `&mut self`, so it could be simplified to `jobs: Option<Sender<Job>>`.

title: rational_le reimplemented byte-for-byte in check.rs and ltl.rs because Rational lacks an Ord impl
proof: Personally re-read check.rs:39-42 and ltl.rs:52-58. Both `rational_le` bodies are byte-identical: `i128::from(a.numerator()) * i128::from(b.denominator()) <= i128::from(b.numerator()) * i128::from(a.denominator())`. check.rs:36-38 doc admits the duplication ("matching `ltl.rs`'s comparison"). Root cause confirmed at types.rs:110: `#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)] pub struct Rational` — no `PartialOrd`/`Ord` and no inherent comparison method (grep of types.rs found none). Both modules hand-roll the same private free `rational_le`; the value comparison belongs once on the type. Idiomatic-Rust violation (DRY / comparison-on-the-type) is real and low-severity.
suggested_fix: Do NOT implement `PartialOrd`/`Ord` for `Rational`: types.rs:123-138 (`Rational::new`) stores numerator/denominator as-given and does NOT reduce to lowest terms (only `from_f64` reduces, per its doc at types.rs:150), so the derived `PartialEq`/`Eq` is STRUCTURAL (`1/2 != 2/4`). A value-based cross-multiply `Ord` would report `Equal` for `1/2` vs `2/4` while `Eq` reports them unequal — that breaks Rust's `Ord`/`Eq` consistency contract and silently corrupts `BTreeMap`/`BTreeSet`/`sort`. Instead add ONE inherent value-comparison method (e.g. `Rational::le` or a documented value-`cmp`) in a single module and call it from both check.rs and ltl.rs, deleting the duplicate `rational_le`.

title: Lying comment: enrichment events classified as 'not yet emitted by this client' but they ARE emitted
proof: Personally re-read log.rs:146-154. The block header (146) reads `// --- Vocabulary completeness (not yet emitted by this client) ---` and the comment (147-149) says enrichment events "arrive with the client-side violation-enrichment slice (R4b)" — future tense. Immediately below, log.rs:152 defines `ENRICHMENT_PROPERTY_INDEX_OOB` and log.rs:154 `ENRICHMENT_EXTRACTION_FAILED`. Contradicting emit sites verified in lib.rs: line 359 emits `events::ENRICHMENT_PROPERTY_INDEX_OOB` (in the OOB branch of the enrichment-todo builder); lines 406 and 479 emit `events::ENRICHMENT_EXTRACTION_FAILED` (in `diag_values` and the end-of-stream path). The R4b slice is shipped: enrich.rs:4 module doc reads "Client-side violation enrichment (Slice R4b).". So both constants are live, emitted events misclassified under the 'not yet emitted' header — a lying comment. The sibling `cache.*` / `extraction.*` constants in the same block (log.rs:156-164) genuinely are not emitted, so only the two enrichment constants are mislabeled.
suggested_fix: Move `ENRICHMENT_PROPERTY_INDEX_OOB` and `ENRICHMENT_EXTRACTION_FAILED` into the `--- Emitted by this client ---` group and mark each `*(emitted)*` (like log.rs:127 `DBC_PARSED`). Narrow the 'not yet emitted' comment to cover only the `cache.*` / `extraction.*` events that truly are unimplemented in this binding. (Rust-internal: Go documents both as emitted at go/aletheia/doc.go:83-84 and emits them at client.go:989/1056; the comment defect is specific to Rust's log.rs vocabulary block.)

title: Asymmetric DRY: extended-flag encode copy-pasted across 7 sites while decode has a helper
proof: Personally re-read dbc.rs and grepped every `extended` occurrence. Decode side has ONE helper: dbc.rs:488 `fn extended_flag(t: &Value) -> bool { t.get("extended").and_then(Value::as_bool).unwrap_or(false) }`, reused at 500, 504, 579, 583, 592, 597 (matched the cited reuse set within rounding of one line). Encode side repeats the inverse block at exactly 7 sites, each `if … { o["extended"] = json!(true); }`: 772-773 (message, reads `if m.extended`), 784-785 and 795-796 (comment_target_to_value Message/Signal), 862-863, 873-874, 881-882, 893-894 (attr_target_to_value Message/Signal/NodeMsg/NodeSig). No shared encode helper mirrors `extended_flag`. The encode/decode asymmetry and 7-way copy are exactly as cited; this is a drift-prone DRY/separation-of-concerns defect (one arm could be missed when the wire convention changes).
suggested_fix: Add an encode helper `fn set_extended(o: &mut Value, extended: bool) { if extended { o["extended"] = json!(true); } }` and call it from all 7 sites, mirroring the decode-side `extended_flag` so each direction's wire convention lives in one place.

title: Duplicated diag-signal filter predicate in diag_values and enrich_eos
proof: Personally re-read lib.rs:398-402 and lib.rs:469-473. In `diag_values`: `all.iter().filter(|(n, _)| diag.signals.iter().any(|s| s == n)).cloned().collect()`. In the end-of-stream path: `merged.iter().filter(|(n, _)| diag.signals.iter().any(|s| s == n)).cloned().collect()`. The two differ only in the source binding name (`all` vs `merged`); the `.filter(|(n, _)| diag.signals.iter().any(|s| s == n)).cloned().collect()` tail is identical. A future change to the diag-signal matching rule must be made in two places — a copy-paste DRY defect.
suggested_fix: Extract one helper `fn select_diag_values(diag: &PropertyDiagnostic, values: &[(String, Rational)]) -> Vec<(String, Rational)>` and call it from both the streaming `diag_values` path and the end-of-stream enrichment path.

title: decode_stream doc claims a 'status:"complete"' contract it never enforces — Go AND C++ peers DO enforce it (parity divergence)
proof: Personally re-read response.rs:451-471. Doc (451): `/// Decode an `end_stream` (`status:"complete"`) response.` Body (452-471) calls `parse_object`, then reads ONLY `results` (via `property_list`) and `warnings` — there is NO `obj.get("status")` check. The doc-vs-code mismatch holds exactly. Sibling strict decoders verified: `decode_ack_or_success` (response.rs:418-419) requires `Some("ack" | "success")` with the rationale "so response-shape drift surfaces" (415-417); `decode_frame` (432) keys on status==ack; `decode_validation` (543) requires `Some("validation")`; `decode_format_text` (557) requires `Some("success")`. CROSS-BINDING (the headline): both peers enforce the status Rust documents but skips — Go `parseStreamResponse` at go/aletheia/json.go:1260-1262 returns a protocol error when `status != "complete"`, and C++ at cpp/src/json_parse.cpp:924-925 returns `std::unexpected(make_error(Protocol, "Expected complete response"))` when `status != "complete"`. The core DOES emit `status:"complete"` (confirmed by go/aletheia/stream_test.go fixtures), so the doc's contract is accurate and Rust silently accepts a non-complete response both peers reject. The peer `decode_extraction` (response.rs:474-531) is also status-lenient, but the provable defect here is scoped to `decode_stream`.
suggested_fix: Enforce the documented contract in `decode_stream`: `match obj.get("status").and_then(Value::as_str) { Some("complete") => …, other => Err(protocol(format!("expected complete response, got {other:?}"))) }`, matching Go (json.go:1260-1262), C++ (json_parse.cpp:924-925), and r24's drift-surfacing convention. Separately surface to the maintainer the parallel `decode_extraction` (response.rs:474) leniency as the same direction call — do not author it as a new finding.

title: FfiBackend re-resolves every FFI symbol via dlsym on every call (2 dlsym + 2 dlerror per streamed frame), where Go and C++ cache them once at construction
proof: Independently re-verified. backend.rs:438-439 `invoke` resolves `aletheia_free_str` on EVERY string-returning call before running the op closure (`let free_str = unsafe { symbol::<...>(lib, b"aletheia_free_str\0") }?;`). The op itself is resolved INSIDE the closure passed to `invoke`: send_frame_binary backend.rs:476 `let f = unsafe { symbol::<SendFrameFn>(lib, b"aletheia_send_frame\0") }?;` — so the per-frame streaming path (send_frame at lib.rs:666 → send_frame_binary at lib.rs:678 → invoke) performs 2 symbol resolutions per frame. send_error/send_remote/start_stream/end_stream/format_dbc/extract_signals/process all likewise re-resolve op+free_str per call. build_frame_bin (backend.rs:565-567) and update_frame_bin (backend.rs:607-609) bypass `invoke` and re-resolve their op + aletheia_free_str per call. `symbol` (backend.rs:236-237) is `lib.get(name)`; verified libloading 0.8.9 (Cargo.lock pinned) Library::get (os/unix/mod.rs:268) → get_singlethreaded (line 284/312) → get_impl (line 210) which at line 225 runs `dlsym(self.handle, symbol.as_ptr())` AND at line 224 a preceding `dlerror()` — so the true per-frame cost is 2 dlsym + 2 dlerror, even more than the finding stated. No per-call heap alloc: cstr_cow_from_bytes (util.rs:16) returns Cow::Borrowed for the NUL-terminated literal, verified. Library is loaded once into a process-global OnceLock and never unloaded (backend.rs:208-209, 215-229), so the resolved pointer is identical on every call — pure repeated work. Peers cache once: Go ffi.go:301 resolves sendFrameFn at init, stored as struct field (ffi.go:194/388) and called on the hot path as b.sendFrameFn / b.freeStrFn (ffi.go:495/509); C++ resolves send_frame_fn_/free_str_fn_ once in the constructor (ffi_backend.cpp:182-183) and calls send_frame_fn_(...) directly on the hot path (ffi_backend.cpp:316). DOWNGRADED to low: the defect, the parity divergence, and the violation of the documented hot-path discipline (CLAUDE.md: avoid avoidable per-call work on the streaming path) are all real and trivially fixable, but the dlsym/dlerror lookups are fast and dominated by the full GHC-RTS FFI round-trip they precede, so the practical efficiency impact on the streaming hot path is small — not the medium-tier impact claimed.
suggested_fix: Resolve all required symbols once in FfiBackend::new (as Go ffi.go:289-349 and C++ ffi_backend.cpp:182-183 do) and store them as typed function-pointer fields on FfiBackend, including the free_str deallocator. Each method calls the cached pointer directly. Because libloading Symbol borrows the 'static Library in the OnceLock, store the resolved Symbol<T> (or raw fn pointers via Symbol::into_raw/deref). This drops the per-frame cost from 2 dlsym+2 dlerror to 0 and matches the peer bindings' init-once contract.

title: enrich_eos merges last-frame signal values with an O(N^2) linear-scan dedup where Go uses a hash set
proof: Independently re-verified. lib.rs:454 `let mut merged: Vec<(String, Rational)> = Vec::new();` then lib.rs:459-463 inside the per-frame loop: `for (name, value) in found { if !merged.iter().any(|(n, _)| n == &name) { merged.push((name, value)); } }`. The `merged.iter().any(...)` is a full linear scan of the accumulated vector per inserted name → O(S^2) string comparisons over S distinct signal names. Go's analogue uses a set: enrich.go:174 `seen := make(map[SignalName]bool)`, threaded through collectSignalsInto with O(1) membership at enrich.go:184 `if !seen[name]`, and the value collection is keyed `Signals map[SignalName]PhysicalValue` (enrich.go:35). The same linear-scan pattern recurs at enrich.rs:68 `if !out.iter().any(|x| x == s)` versus Go's seen-map. Honest magnitude confirmed: this runs only at EOS (enrich_eos at lib.rs:435, not the per-frame hot path), and the same loop performs one FFI extract_all round-trip per distinct frame (lib.rs:457 → extract_all at lib.rs:328), so the quadratic string-compare is dominated by the unavoidable per-frame FFI extraction cost. Real, trivially avoidable, small in practice — low severity as the finder rated.
suggested_fix: Track seen names in a HashSet<String> (or HashSet<&str>) alongside `merged` to make the dedup O(1) per name, matching Go's `seen map[SignalName]bool`; optionally apply the same to enrich.rs collect_into. Low priority because the EOS path is dominated by the O(N) FFI extractions in the same loop.

title: C++ silently sign-flips a negative-denominator rational on DBC decode; Python/Go/Rust all reject it
proof: Re-verified all four bindings. C++ parse_rational_dict (cpp/src/json_parse.cpp:213-217) accepts and rewrites a negative denominator: `if (den < 0) { if (num == ...::min()) throw...; num = -num; den = -den; }` — silent canonicalization. The helper is reached for every DBC signal numeric: parse_rational (json_parse.cpp:275-283) -> parse_rational_dict, called by parse_signal_def for factor/offset/minimum/maximum (json_parse.cpp:350-353), and also parse_signal_value (227-237) and parse_rational_as_int (285-298). All three peers REJECT den<0 and each documents the wire-symmetry rationale in-comment: Go parseRational (go/aletheia/json.go:676-678) `if den < 0 { return ...protocolError("negative denominator in rational") }` with comment 670-675 'silently rewriting here would let asymmetric wire shapes pass through ... per feedback_cross_binding_wire_symmetry.md'; Rust Rational::new (rust/src/types.rs:129-133) returns Error::Validation when `denominator < 0` with doc-comment 'the core and the other bindings reject non-positive denominators at the wire rather than silently rewriting them'; Python extract_rational_from_dict (python/aletheia/client/_helpers/rational.py:108-110) raises ProtocolError when `denominator <= 0`, docstring (90-94) 'A negative denominator on the wire would otherwise be silently sign-flipped by Fraction(n, d), hiding the wire-format violation.' C++ does exactly the thing three peers explicitly guard against, and is the lone outlier (3-1). Severity stays medium (not low like 2-6) BECAUSE three peers carry an explicit in-comment reject-CONTRACT for precisely this case citing a cross-binding rule — a deliberately-ratified invariant C++ violates, not merely a stricter-elsewhere leniency.
suggested_fix: In parse_rational_dict, reject `den < 0` with a runtime_error (matching the existing `den == 0` throw at 218-219) instead of normalizing it, so C++ joins the Python/Go/Rust 'reject non-positive denominator' wire contract documented in feedback_cross_binding_wire_symmetry.md.

title: Rust does not validate DBC environment-variable varType on decode; Python/Go/C++ all reject values outside {0,1,2}
proof: Re-verified. Rust decode_env_var (rust/src/dbc.rs:636-644) stores `var_type: i64_field(e, "varType")?` into the unconstrained `pub var_type: i64` (dbc.rs:152) with NO membership check; i64_field (dbc.rs:398-402) only checks the value is an integer. Any i64 (e.g. 7) round-trips back out unchecked at the serializer (dbc.rs:811 `"varType": e.var_type`). The three peers all validate {0,1,2}: C++ parse_env_var_type (cpp/src/json_parse.cpp:424-435) switches 0/1/2 and `throw std::runtime_error("Unknown environment variable type ...")` in the default arm; Go parseEnvironmentVars (go/aletheia/json.go:1607-1608) `if tagVal < 0 || tagVal > 2 { return ...protocolError("unknown varType tag: %d") }`; Python _normalize_var_type (python/aletheia/client/_helpers/dbc_normalize.py:145-148) raises ProtocolError when `raw not in (0,1,2)`, and the TypedDict pins DBCVarType = Literal[0,1,2] (python/aletheia/_dbc_types.py:131). Genuine 3-1 split, Rust the lone outlier. Low: the Agda core only ever emits 0/1/2 (varTypeToℕ), so this is reachable only on hand-built/adversarial wire input.
suggested_fix: Add a {0,1,2} membership check in Rust decode_env_var (map to a typed VarType enum, or return Error::Validation/protocol for any other value), mirroring C++ parse_env_var_type / Go's tag check / Python's Literal[0,1,2] domain.

title: Rust AND Python do not validate CAN-id range or DLC byte-count on DBC message decode; Go/C++ do (2-2 split, not Rust-only)
proof: Defect confirmed but the finder's 'Rust lone outlier' cross-binding claim is WRONG — Python is equally lenient. Rust decode_message (rust/src/dbc.rs:474,480) stores `id: u32_field(msg, "id")?` / `dlc: u32_field(msg, "dlc")?` where u32_field (rust/src/response.rs:345-350) only checks u32 fit — no 11-/29-bit id range, no DLC-set {0..8,12,16,20,24,32,48,64} membership; fields are bare `pub id: u32` / `pub dlc: u32` (dbc.rs:104,110); can_id() (dbc.rs:962-970) is a lazy optional accessor the decoder never calls and there is no dlc validator at all. Go parseDBCMessage (go/aletheia/json.go:1935-1965) rejects negative id, range-checks uint32, routes through NewStandardID/NewExtendedID, and calls BytesToDLC; C++ parse_message_def (cpp/src/json_parse.cpp:383-411) routes id through json_to_can_id (365-381, throws when standard id > 0x7FF) and dlc through bytes_to_dlc (388-390, throws on invalid DLC set). BUT Python normalize_dbc (python/aletheia/client/_helpers/dbc_normalize.py:506-523) builds each message as `msg_dict = dict(m); msg_dict["signals"] = signals` — id/dlc pass through UNVALIDATED; DBCMessage types `id: int` (plain) and `dlc: DLCByteCount` where DLCByteCount = NewType("DLCByteCount", int) (_dbc_types.py:27) is type-level-only with no runtime check. So the real split is 2-2 (Go+C++ validate; Rust+Python do not), not single-binding. Downgraded from the implied 'Rust outlier' framing to a 2-2 divergence; severity low (Agda core never emits an out-of-range id/dlc).
suggested_fix: Fix the divergence in lockstep, NOT Rust alone (per the cross-binding consistency rule). Rust decode_message: validate id via the can_id() logic (CanId::standard/extended) and dlc against the legal byte-count set before constructing DbcMessage. Python normalize_dbc: add an equivalent id-range + DLC-set check on the message decode path. Match json_to_can_id/bytes_to_dlc (C++) and NewStandardID/BytesToDLC (Go).

title: Empty/missing multiplex_values: Go rejects both; C++ throws on missing only; Rust AND Python accept silently (four-way, not three-way)
proof: Defect confirmed but the finder's 'three-way' framing omits Python, making it four-way. Go parseSignalPresence (go/aletheia/json.go:2128-2130): `rawVals, ok := j["multiplex_values"].([]any); if !ok || len(rawVals) == 0 { return nil, protocolError("multiplex_values must be a non-empty array") }` — rejects empty AND missing. C++ parse_signal_def (cpp/src/json_parse.cpp:315-322): only reached when `j.contains("multiplexor")`; then `const auto& arr = j.at("multiplex_values")` (316) throws std::out_of_range on a missing key, but the loop (319-320) iterates an empty array silently — throws-on-missing-only. Rust decode_presence (rust/src/dbc.rs:419-431): in the 'multiplexed' arm, `arr(sig, "multiplex_values")` where arr (dbc.rs:385-389) returns `&[]` for a missing/non-array key, so both missing and empty yield `Multiplexed { values: vec![] }` with no error — lenient. Python normalize_signal (python/aletheia/client/_helpers/dbc_normalize.py:114-121) passes the raw signal dict through verbatim (only converting numeric fields to Fraction) — no presence/multiplex validation at all, so empty or missing multiplex_values flows through untouched — lenient. Accurate framing: Go-strict / C++-partial / Rust+Python-lenient. Low: every serializer emits a non-empty multiplex_values for a real multiplexed signal, so reachable only via hand-built objects.
suggested_fix: Pick one contract (Go's reject-empty-and-missing is strictest and matches the semantic that a multiplexed signal needs >=1 selector value) and apply it in all bindings: C++ validate the key exists AND the array is non-empty; Rust error when the multiplex_values slice is empty; Python validate multiplex_values is a non-empty list on the multiplexed branch of normalize_signal.

title: Multiplexed-signal discriminator on decode disagrees across bindings: only Rust keys on the canonical 'presence' field; C++/Go key on 'multiplexor'; Python keys on neither (passthrough)
proof: Defect confirmed; the finder's 'Rust/Python key on presence' is WRONG on Python — corrected here. All four serializers deliberately emit `presence` as the explicit canonical discriminator (R19 cluster 17 / PY-D-19.2): C++ presence_to_json (cpp/src/json_serialize.cpp:72,74), Go (go/aletheia/json.go:100,108), Rust (rust/src/dbc.rs:750,755), and the Python TypedDict documents it (python/aletheia/_dbc_types.py:67-70,83 'presence ... narrowed to Literal["multiplexed"] so consumers can use it as the discriminator'). But the DECODERS diverge four ways: Rust decode_presence (rust/src/dbc.rs:417) `match str_field(sig, "presence").as_str()` — the only binding that actually dispatches on `presence`; C++ parse_signal_def (cpp/src/json_parse.cpp:315) `if (j.contains("multiplexor"))` — never reads `presence`; Go parseSignalPresence (go/aletheia/json.go:2124-2126) `muxName := getString(j, "multiplexor"); if muxName == "" { return AlwaysPresent{}, nil }` — never reads `presence`; Python normalize_signal (python/aletheia/client/_helpers/dbc_normalize.py:114-121) reads NEITHER — it passes the dict through verbatim, and downstream consumers (python/aletheia/dbc/_queries.py:22,32,42 `s.get("multiplexor") is not None`) key on `multiplexor`. Consequence: a wire object with `presence:"multiplexed"` but no `multiplexor` key is decoded as multiplexed by Rust (then errors / keeps empty) but as always-present by C++/Go/Python. Low: every serializer emits `presence` and `multiplexor` together and in agreement, so the disagreement is reachable only on a hand-crafted object where the two fields contradict — real wire data never triggers it.
suggested_fix: Make C++, Go, and Python decode multiplexing by reading the canonical `presence` field ('always' vs 'multiplexed') — matching Rust and the documented Python TypedDict contract — then read multiplexor/multiplex_values only in the 'multiplexed' arm. This aligns each binding's decoder with its own serializer's deliberate R19 explicit-discriminator change.

title: Go decodes rational numerator/denominator through float64, losing precision above 2^53; C++/Rust/Python preserve exact integer precision
proof: Re-verified. Go parseRational (go/aletheia/json.go:652-653) reads `num, ok1 := n["numerator"].(float64); den, ok2 := n["denominator"].(float64)` — float64 is the only available type because the DBC blob is decoded via plain json.Unmarshal into map[string]any (parseResponse, json.go:797-803), and the in-file comment (719-721) confirms 'Go's json.Unmarshal decodes all numbers as float64 (53-bit mantissa), so integers above 2^53 lose precision silently.' parseRational is the DBC signal factor/offset/min/max decoder (called at go/aletheia/json.go:2022-2036). The range guard at 664-665 catches |x| > MaxInt64 but NOT the 2^53..2^63 mantissa-truncation window. Peers are exact: C++ parse_rational_dict (cpp/src/json_parse.cpp:205-206) `j.at("numerator").get<std::int64_t>()`; Rust rational_from_value (rust/src/response.rs:367,371) `Value::as_i64` (serde_json preserves integer tokens exactly); Python extract_rational_from_dict (python/aletheia/client/_helpers/rational.py:96-111) keeps the native JSON int (is_pure_int) and builds Fraction(n,d) exactly. Low: the comment frames it as a documented-acceptable approximation for bounded CAN fields, but the DBC factor/offset/min/max rational is the one unbounded-magnitude path and a genuine cross-binding precision divergence (Go-only).
suggested_fix: Decode the DBC blob with a json.Decoder configured via UseNumber() (or json.Number-typed fields) so numerator/denominator parse as exact integers, matching the exact-int64 contract of the C++/Rust/Python decoders.

title: C++ ExtractionResult.errors is an anonymous std::pair while Go/Rust use a named SignalError type
proof: Re-read cpp/include/aletheia/response.hpp:41 — `std::vector<std::pair<SignalName, std::string>> errors;` inside `struct ExtractionResult`. grep over cpp/ for `SignalError` returns ZERO hits — no named type exists in C++. Both construction sites verified: cpp/src/client.cpp:331 `result.errors.emplace_back(std::move(name), std::move(msg));` (binary path) and cpp/src/json_parse.cpp:778 `errors.emplace_back(SignalName{e.at("name")...}, e.value("error", ""));` (JSON path). A C++ consumer must read `.first`/`.second`, semantically opaque. Peers verified: Go go/aletheia/result.go:12-16 `type SignalError struct { Name SignalName; Error string }` used as `Errors []SignalError` (result.go:23); Rust rust/src/response.rs:94-100 `pub struct SignalError { pub name: String, pub reason: String }` used as `errors: Vec<SignalError>` (response.rs:108). Python is `errors: Mapping[str, str]` (python/aletheia/client/_types.py:340) — a named-FIELD map shape, a different shape from the Go/Rust named-struct list, so the named-struct peer set is Go+Rust and C++ is the lone anonymous outlier among the two structurally-comparable peers. Violates the project's own `feedback_no_opaque_composite_types` rule ('no anon tuple/dict returns; NamedTuple + type aliases') and the r24 precedent that upgraded Rust's representation to a named `SignalError`.
suggested_fix: Introduce `struct SignalError { SignalName name; std::string reason; };` in cpp/include/aletheia/response.hpp and change `errors` to `std::vector<SignalError>`, mirroring Go/Rust. Update the two construction sites (client.cpp:331, json_parse.cpp:778) to aggregate-init the named fields, and any consumers/tests reading `.first`/`.second`.

title: C++ build_frame/update_frame lack the client-side "no DBC message for CAN ID" guard that Go and Python apply before the signal loop
proof: Re-read cpp/src/client.cpp:411 (build_frame) and :431 (update_frame): each guards only `if (signal_index_.empty())` (the no-DBC-at-all case) before calling `resolve_signals(id, signals)` (defined at client.cpp:381-405). resolve_signals checks `signal_index_.find(SignalKey{id_value,is_extended,signal_name})` ONLY inside the per-signal `for (const auto& sv : signals)` loop (lines 391-403); with empty `signals` the loop body never executes and an unknown CAN ID is never detected — the call proceeds to `resolve->injection()` (count=0) and reaches `backend_->build_frame_bin(...)`. So at the client level C++ has no message-existence guard; with empty signals an unknown CAN ID passes the client uncaught. Peers reject this client-side before the loop: Go go/aletheia/client.go:234-237 `indexMap, ok := c.signalIndex[key]; if !ok { return validationError("%s: no DBC message for CAN ID %d ...") }`; Python python/aletheia/client/_client.py:287-293 `lookup = self._signal_lookup.get((can_id, extended)); if lookup is None: ... raise ValidationError("... no DBC message for CAN ID ...")` — both BEFORE iterating signals. Note: C++ already maintains a message-keyed index `signal_names_` keyed by `MessageKey{id_value,is_extended}` (client.hpp:280-281, populated in parse_dbc at client.cpp:154), so the lookup needed for the guard already exists. Severity low (narrow edge: only empty-signals + unknown-CAN-ID reaches the gap; non-empty signals still error per-signal). I did NOT verify the FFI/core behavior on count=0, so I scope the proof to the client-side parity gap, not to a definite 'succeeds' outcome.
suggested_fix: In C++ resolve_signals (or in build_frame/update_frame before the loop) look up whether any message index entry exists for `{id_value, is_extended}` (e.g. via the existing `signal_names_` map) and return `AletheiaError{ErrorKind::Validation, std::format("... no DBC message for CAN ID {}", id_value)}` when none does, matching Go (client.go:236) and Python (_client.py:292).

title: Rust build_frame/update_frame take &DbcMessage; Go/C++/Python take a CAN ID and resolve via an internal index
proof: The signature divergence is real and verified: rust/src/lib.rs:794-799 `pub fn build_frame(&self, message: &DbcMessage, dlc: Dlc, signals: &[SignalValue])` and lib.rs:821-827 update_frame likewise take `message: &DbcMessage`, resolving via `resolve_signal_indices(message, signals)` (lib.rs:116-133, positional match in `message.signals`). Peers take a CAN ID resolved against a client-internal index: Go client.go:509 `BuildFrame(ctx, id CANID, ...)` → resolveSignalIndices(...,id); C++ client.cpp:407 `build_frame(stop, CanId id, ...)`; Python build_frame(can_id, ...). HOWEVER the finding is downgraded from a parity DEFECT to a low DOC-NIT because two of its load-bearing claims are REFUTED: (1) the claim 'was accepted in the Rust r24 review' is FALSE — I read all 10 r24 findings (.archive/reviews/r24/findings/*.yaml) and none concerns build_frame/update_frame signatures or DbcMessage; (2) the claim that Rust 'cannot express the no-DBC-message-for-CAN-ID diagnostic … it is structurally precluded' is FALSE — `Dbc::message_by_id(&self, id: CanId) -> Option<&DbcMessage>` (rust/src/dbc.rs:1048) returns None for an unknown CAN ID, so that diagnostic is expressible one call earlier, just relocated. Against the project PRINCIPLE (MEMORY feedback_cross_language_parity: 'same surface across bindings … idiomatic shape flexible; match the feature not the signature'), `&DbcMessage` delivers the same feature the Rust way and is principle-permitted. The surviving kernel: FEATURE_MATRIX.yaml rows 255-271 mark Rust build_frame/update_frame only `status: implemented` with no note of the `&DbcMessage` divergence, so it could be misread as drift.
suggested_fix: Add a note to docs/FEATURE_MATRIX.yaml's build_frame/update_frame Rust rows (lines 262, 271) recording that Rust takes `&DbcMessage` by design (typed idiom; resolves positionally in message.signals; the no-message case is surfaced one call earlier via `Dbc::message_by_id` returning None), so it is not read as parity drift. No code change required.

title: C++ check within() lacks the ms→µs overflow guard that Go and Rust both apply (signed-int64 UB)
proof: Verified personally. cpp/include/aletheia/check.hpp:141-144 (SettlesBuilder::within): `auto within(std::chrono::milliseconds ms) const -> CheckResult { if (ms.count() < 0) throw std::invalid_argument("time must be non-negative"); auto us = std::chrono::duration_cast<Timestamp>(ms);` — the ONLY guard is `< 0`. Identical site at check.hpp:220-223 (ThenCondition::within). A grep of the whole header confirms these are the only two duration_cast<Timestamp> conversion sites (no third with-or-without a guard). Timestamp = std::chrono::microseconds (cpp/include/aletheia/types.hpp:233). duration_cast<microseconds>(milliseconds) multiplies count() by 1000 in the common rep (long long / int64): for ms.count() > INT64_MAX/1000 (~9.2e15) this is signed-integer overflow = undefined behavior. Reachability is established by the PUBLIC API alone: `check::signal(name).settles_between(lo,hi).within(std::chrono::milliseconds{huge})` (cpp/include/aletheia/check.hpp:197-200 → 141) reaches the unguarded cast directly; no caller bounds the ms first. PEERS GUARD: Go check.go:200 (SettlesBuilder.Within) and check.go:344 (ThenCondition.Within) `if timeMs > math.MaxInt64/usPerMillisecond { return CheckResult{}, validationError("time %d ms overflows microsecond conversion") }`; Rust check.rs:45-49 `ms.checked_mul(US_PER_MS).ok_or_else(|| Error::Validation(format!("time {ms} ms overflows microsecond conversion")))`, tested at check.rs:597 `signal("S").settles_between(0,10).within(u64::MAX).is_err()`. Python is unaffected (unbounded ints). No C++ test asserts within-ms overflow rejection (none in cpp/tests/). FEATURE_MATRIX declares check_dsl + yaml_check_loader implemented across bindings.
suggested_fix: Before the duration_cast at check.hpp:144 and check.hpp:223, reject overflow the way the peers do: `if (ms.count() > std::chrono::milliseconds::max().count()/1000) throw std::invalid_argument("time " + std::to_string(ms.count()) + " ms overflows microsecond conversion");` (or fold the guard into ltl::within/always_within). Add a C++ test mirroring Rust check.rs:597 and the Go overflow test.

title: C++ inline-YAML loader has no input-length bound, unlike the file loader and all three peers' inline YAML loaders
proof: Verified personally. cpp/src/yaml.cpp:259-266: `auto load_checks_from_yaml_string(std::string_view yaml) -> Result<std::vector<CheckResult>> { try { auto root = YAML::Load(std::string(yaml)); return parse_yaml_checks(root); } catch (const YAML::Exception& ex) { ... } }` — zero size check on the caller-supplied string. By contrast the C++ FILE loader at cpp/src/yaml.cpp:242-249 calls `detail::validate_loader_path(path, "YAML")` then `detail::check_file_size_bound(path)`, and check_file_size_bound (cpp/src/detail/loader_utils.cpp:201-218) rejects `size > max_dbc_text_bytes` with ErrorKind::InputBoundExceeded. So the inline path is the lone unbounded YAML entry point in C++. PEERS BOUND THE INLINE PATH: Python yaml_loader.py:189 `check_dbc_text_size_bound(len(source.encode()))` on the str branch; Go yaml.go:107-114 inline branch `if size := uint64(len(source)); size > MaxDBCTextBytes { return nil, &InputBoundExceededError{...} }`; Rust yaml.rs:51-64 `load_checks_from_yaml` → `load_checks_within(content, MAX_INPUT_BYTES)` which checks `if content.len() > limit { return Err(invalid(...)) }`. The bound is the same 64 MiB across bindings (Rust MAX_INPUT_BYTES = 64*1024*1024 yaml.rs:31; Go MaxDBCTextBytes = 64*1024*1024 limits.go:36; C++ max_dbc_text_bytes used by the file loader). SPEC: AGENTS.md:44 names "YAML check loaders" as a trust boundary that "must enforce explicit upper bounds: ... max total input length" and says "The first review round under this rule must surface every parser entry point that has no bound check." No C++ test exercises an oversize inline YAML rejection.
suggested_fix: Add a size-bound check at the top of load_checks_from_yaml_string mirroring the peers / the file loader: reject `yaml.size() > detail::max_dbc_text_bytes` with `AletheiaError{ErrorKind::InputBoundExceeded, ..., ErrorCode::InputBoundExceeded, InputBoundExceededError{...}}` before calling YAML::Load.

title: C++ ExtractionResult.errors is a bare std::pair, while Python/Go/Rust use a named SignalError type
proof: Verified personally. cpp/include/aletheia/response.hpp:41: `std::vector<std::pair<SignalName, std::string>> errors;` — an anonymous heterogeneous pair where the consumer must remember .first = name, .second = reason. The project ALREADY adjudicated this exact shape: the r24 Rust review changed ExtractionResult.errors → Vec<SignalError> (CLAUDE.md / MEMORY record "ExtractionResult.errors→Vec<SignalError>"), and Rust now exposes a named struct response.rs:95-100 `pub struct SignalError { pub name: String, pub reason: String }`. The other two peers were already named: Go result.go:13-15 `type SignalError struct { Name SignalName; Error string }` with `Errors []SignalError` (result.go:23); Python types.py:460 `class SignalError(TypedDict)` (name/error fields) with `errors: list[SignalError]` (types.py:649). So C++ is the lone holdout using an anonymous pair. SPEC: feedback_no_opaque_composite_types ("No anonymous heterogeneous tuple/dict return types — name the parts"). Sub-note: even among the named peers the field name diverges — Rust `reason` vs Go/Python `error` — so the C++ fix should also align the field name across all four.
suggested_fix: Introduce `struct SignalError { SignalName name; std::string reason; };` in cpp/include/aletheia/response.hpp and change errors to `std::vector<SignalError>`, matching the r24 Rust precedent and the Go/Python peers. Pick one field name (`reason` or `error`) and apply it consistently across all four bindings in the same change (CHANGELOG entry required — public-header signature change).

title: C++ IssueCode::Unknown drops the original wire code, while Python/Go/Rust preserve it
proof: Verified personally. cpp/src/json_parse.cpp:266-271 `parse_issue_code(std::string_view s)` scans issue_code_table and returns the payload-less `IssueCode::Unknown` for any unrecognized code. cpp/src/json_parse.cpp:1036-1040 `to_string(IssueCode code)` scans the table and `return "unknown";` for it — the original wire code is irrecoverably lost. The C++ IssueCode is a plain `enum class` (validation.hpp:13-36) and ValidationIssue (validation.hpp:38-42) has only {severity, code, detail} — no field stashes the raw code, so it is not preserved elsewhere. PEERS PRESERVE: Rust response.rs has `Unknown(String)` (declared response.rs:55-56) — from_str maps `other => IssueCode::Unknown(other.to_string())` (~line 86) and as_str returns `IssueCode::Unknown(s) => s` (~line 115), so the original round-trips; Go IssueCode is `type IssueCode string` (result.go:164) and json.go:985 `code := IssueCode(getString(issue, "code"))` casts the raw wire string verbatim (an unknown `"future_code"` stays IssueCode("future_code")); Python ValidationIssue is a TypedDict whose `code` field is just the raw dict value — _response_parsers.py:142 `cast("list[ValidationIssue]", list(warnings_field))` is a runtime no-op, so the wire string passes through unchanged (the IssueCode StrEnum annotation is not runtime-enforced). Thus an unknown code `"future_code"` survives in all 3 peers but becomes "unknown" in C++. FEATURE_MATRIX validation_errors notes "unknown wire codes → IssueCode::Unknown, forward-compatible" (docs/FEATURE_MATRIX.yaml:444) — C++'s payload-less Unknown does not deliver that forward-compat (the code is not recoverable). NOTE: error.hpp:42 calls Unknown a "forward-compatibility sentinel" — this is NOT a lying comment; it accurately describes graceful-degradation-on-unknown (C++ does not reject unknown codes), which is a weaker property than raw-code preservation; the finding stands as an INCONSISTENT defect, not a lie.
suggested_fix: Give C++ a forward-compatible path: either store the raw wire string in ValidationIssue (e.g. add `std::string raw_code` populated whenever code == IssueCode::Unknown, and have to_string return it for that case) or carry the string on the enum equivalent, so an unknown code round-trips like the three peers. CHANGELOG entry required if ValidationIssue's public shape changes.
